Ideologiemaschinen 
00:00:01
Good evening and thank you very much for the nice introduction from Till. As the 
title suggests, today I would like to present my new book "Ideology Machines". But I changed the 
subtitle a bit. So it is not “How Cancel Culture Works” but “On Dissolving Boundaries in Political 
Communication”. I would like to present a slightly broader framework here. The book actually fits 
in quite well with our lecture series "Digitalization and Society", in that I would like to explain the 
phenomenon of cancel culture as a media effect.
00:00:42
Due to the media change from an analog to a digital media world, and this is my 
crucial thesis, there is a general blurring of boundaries in political communication, as a result of 
which institutions are turning into ideological machines. Such a thesis raises a whole series of 
questions. So questions like, what is political communication? What does politics have to do with 
ideologies? And what exactly is this mechanism that allows political communication today to 
expand into spheres where it is usually not present, namely into the spheres of art, science, and 
education?
00:01:29
And finally, can and should we do anything against such a political dissolution of 
boundaries or against a dissolution of boundaries in political communication? Or shouldn't it be 
welcomed? My lecture will be divided into three parts. I will first introduce you to a theory of 
political communication. On the basis of this theory I will then develop the idea of “ideology 
machines” and finally say a few words about systems therapy. My book takes its starting point from
an ideological theory by the British political scientist Michael Freeden.
00:02:17
With Freeden, I assume that there is a large but limited reservoir of policy ideas. 
Ideas such as equality, freedom, democracy, progress, tolerance, authority, pluralism and many 
more. These political ideas now have two properties, namely first: every political idea has several 
dimensions of meaning. Freedom can mean a free market economy or the free development of the 
subject. Or it can mean free trade zones or the tax allowance or even free travel for free citizens. 
The second point is that these political ideas never exist on their own, but rather that they define 
themselves relationally to each other and thus limit their original scope of meaning.
00:03:14
There is an irreducible plurality of such political ideas that are initially of equal rank 
and value and that are not subject to any natural hierarchy. Equality is no better than freedom. 
Freedom is no worse than democracy. Democracy is not more important than peace. And so on and 
so forth. So there are no good reasons. Or rather, there are no non-political reasons to prioritize a 
political idea over other political ideas. But politics does exactly that. It selects a few ideas from the 
large reservoir of political ideas and turns them into core concepts of political ideologies.
00:04:04
Each term is now given a very specific meaning based on its relationship to other 
political terms. Stable conceptual networks and contexts of reasoning emerge, each of which forms 
their own language games. And of course Freeden takes this idea from Ludwig Wittgenstein's 
language game theory. With the help of this concept of ideology, very different party families can 
now be described and differentiated. Equality, justice, security and solidarity are core political 
concepts that constitute the ideology of the social democratic party family.
00:04:58
Central to this is an idea of justice, which essentially means social justice. 
Accordingly, equality is primarily understood as a form of equality of results, such as equal pay for 
equal work or equal living conditions. In this political language game, when you hear the word 
security, you don't first think of internal security or food security, as other parties would do, but 
rather of social security and secure jobs. By forming such networks of core political concepts that 
fix the meanings of political ideas, ideologies gain a property that Freeden describes with a made-up
word, namely decontestation.

00:05:47
In English, deontestation means something like argument or dispute, so 
decontestation can be translated as making something undeniable. Ideologies are language games 
that are defined by a selection of core political terms and are therefore indisputable. The impression 
of indisputability arises, on the one hand, from the logical consistency of these conceptual 
networks. The specific connections and meaning restrictions between the core terms are repeated, 
practiced and confirmed day in and day out in the political communication of a party, a political 
organization or an activist movement - until they appear indisputable and self-evident to those who 
speak this language game.
00:06:39
In addition, these language games appeal to certain realities of life and the political 
interests associated with them and inscribed in them. In a trade union environment, for example, the
configuration of justice, equality, security and solidarity outlined here is simply self-evident. A 
completely different configuration can be observed in the liberal party family. The central core 
concept here is not justice, but freedom. And this concept of freedom is in turn specified in a very 
special way, namely as individual freedom.
00:07:18
But if you view individualism as a positive value, then it is also essential that you 
affirm pluralism, i.e. the pluralism of opinions, world views, lifestyles and cultures. But tolerance 
towards differing opinions is already established as a political value. So you can see here too that 
the definition of a core political concept acts like a magnet and attracts other political ideas and 
determines their meanings. In the Christian, conservative party family there is an intrinsic 
preference for tradition, for family, for security, whereby when one thinks of security one primarily 
thinks of internal security and/or national security.
00:08:13
The concept of freedom also plays a role in this language game. However, this is less
associated with individual freedom and more with the free market economy. The fact that individual
freedom is of secondary importance in this language game is a direct consequence of the fact that 
individualism in the conservative party family is restricted and limited by the values of the family, 
tradition or religion. In addition, authority as a political idea is integrated and woven into the 
conservative language game, so that the authority of the state, the authority of teachers, the police 
and parents are available as a justification resource for this ideology.
00:09:03
The Green Party initially started out as a one-issue party, for which nature 
conservation and environmental protection were the guiding principles. Anyone who wants to take 
on government responsibility and then co-govern must speak out on all sorts of political issues in 
parliament and in public and represent a certain position on them. In this respect, the Greens also 
expanded their language game in the following years and also integrated other core concepts, such 
as peace and security. Peace was initially a kind of secondary idea for the Greens because this party 
had also emerged from the peace movement.
00:09:44
In 1999, however, the Greens gave up their pacifist position in the face of the threat 
of genocide in Kosovo. And this relativization of the idea of peace enables the Greens today to 
speak out in favor of military support for Ukraine, which was actually not a given. Today, this 
family of parties also has a very complex ideology with many mutually limiting core political 
concepts. Populist parties are an interesting special case. They get by with a minimal ideological 
concept that is based on the opposition between elite and people.
00:10:32
Populist parties do not simply reject democracy, as is often falsely claimed, but they 
represent a different concept of democracy, namely a different concept of democracy than liberal 
democracies. The political idea of the demos is absolute and cannot be restricted by any other 
political values. Populism is often described as a thin ideology in political science. Precisely 

because populism only has a small ideological core, it can combine relatively freely and 
opportunistically with all kinds of ideologies - right-wing, left-wing, green, nationalist or 
libertarian.
00:11:20
And that's why there are both right-wing populist and left-wing populist parties. 
Ideologies can also be distinguished according to which political ideas play a central role in their 
language games and which remain peripheral. But because different ideologies use the same core 
political terms but give them different meanings, politicians constantly talk past each other and 
insist on their undeniable positions. So much for Freeden's theory of ideology, which I would like to
expand and supplement - and at one point also criticize - in the following.
00:12:06
One can distinguish between narrow and broad as well as strong and weak 
ideologies. On the horizontal axis, I take up the distinction between "thin and thick ideologies", 
although in German I would prefer to speak of narrow and wide ideologies rather than of thin and 
thick ideologies. Narrow ideologies are language games that make do with just a few core political 
terms. They are characteristic of populist parties, of single-issue parties, of protest movements and 
of political activism in general.
00:12:48
But even the libertarians in the USA, who want to limit the role of the state to the 
bare minimum, only seem to need a minimalist ideology. Broad ideologies, on the other hand, 
integrate many core political concepts and link them together. They are characteristic of liberal 
parties because liberal parties not only gave rise to the idea of liberal democracy, but actually also 
reflect it within themselves. But popular parties are also usually based on broad ideologies because 
they have to appeal to many different sections of the population and therefore require a pluralism of
ideas.
00:13:34
In addition, ideologies can also be distinguished based on how strongly they 
prioritize certain political ideas. In principle, all radical and extremist movements are based on 
strong ideologies that place a very specific political idea above all other political values. This is the 
only reason why they can come to radical solutions on how society needs to be changed. The 
prioritization therefore already contains the revolution and the overthrow. With the additional 
distinction between narrow and wide and between strong and weak ideologies, you now have four 
possible combinations.
00:14:22
Most politicians always have a variety of key political concepts in mind when they 
communicate politically. In addition, they do not form very strong hierarchies of ideas. These 
normal politicians are confronted with political actors who, in the colloquial sense, would be called 
ideologues. In the case of the Greens, this difference was reflected in the distinction between Realos
and Fundis. Ideologists try to bring all conceivable relevant core political concepts into a 
systematic, logical and argumentative order in which individual political ideas are given very strong
priority.
00:15:11
Marxism would be such an ideology or, today, Critical Race Theory. These would be 
the corresponding examples of strong and broad ideological concepts from which political decisions
can then be deduced as if from a logical system. Such ideologies attempt to regulate all aspects of 
human life and in this sense also have a totalitarian character. You can now also see that populism 
cannot be adequately described as a “thin ideology”. Populists combine narrow ideologies with few 
core concepts, with a strong prioritization of a political concept such as the people.
00:16:01
Only this combination means that populist movements can tip into an illiberal or 
authoritarian direction. If the values of freedom, justice, plurality and tolerance are not 
systematically integrated into a political language game, then they cannot restrict the will of the 

people if, for example, it is directed against minority rights. But most people are not political 
people. Their language is not structured by core political terms. Voters do not need ideologies to 
make their voting decisions.
00:16:42
It is enough that you can observe and compare different parties with different 
ideologies. By comparing ideologies, voters can immediately determine and judge whether this or 
that ideology corresponds to their understanding of the world and whether it represents and 
represents their interests. Only totalitarian states need to indoctrinate their populations. Liberal 
democracies – and that is why they are called liberal – generally do not do this. At this point I 
disagree with Michael Freeden, who assumes that we have all always been ideologues and that we 
cannot communicate non-ideologically.
00:17:27
My objection would be that Freeden can only come to this conclusion because he 
does not systematically distinguish power communication from political communication. Power 
communication does not require ideologies; political communication, however, is not. Anyone who 
communicates in the medium of power says that this or that behavior has positive or negative 
consequences for the other person. Anyone who communicates in the medium of politics argues 
about the meaning of political ideas and thereby makes power options visible to the public, between
which one can then choose.
00:18:06
You can now draw two diagonals into this ideological model. One describes the 
degree of indisputability that ideologies claim. The stronger the prioritization of individual political 
ideas and the more comprehensively an ideology integrates the entirety of all political ideas, the 
more dogmatic, i.e. the more ideological, it appears. If, on the other hand, there is a strong 
prioritization in a set of a few core political terms, then the polemical character and the emotional 
appeal of political communication automatically increases.
00:18:48
That's why Trump was able to shout to his supporters at a campaign rally in 
Pennsylvania last week: "Let's not do any more questions, just let us listen to music. Let's make it 
into music." Instead of giving a campaign speech and answering questions from citizens, Trump 
simply played his audience a playlist of his favorite songs for half an hour. This is also political 
communication. If you combine narrow ideologies with strong claims to validity, you can also 
reduce politics to music. And here I would like to thank Stefan Bönrchen, who gave me this 
unbeatable example.
00:19:38
One thing is particularly important for the argument that follows. We now have a 
clear criterion by which political communication can be distinguished from other forms of 
communication, including communication in science, education and art. Political communication is 
based on language games that make themselves undeniable and that trigger arguments when the 
self-limited meanings of their political ideas are questioned. This is the real problem when political 
communication is becoming increasingly unbounded and a political communication style is 
becoming dominant in the institutions of science, education and art.
00:20:22
And this brings me to the central thesis of my book, namely that institutions are 
transforming into ideological machines. If you want to explain how such politicization occurs, you 
first need an institutional model that can be used to understand how institutions work in general. 
And secondly, you also have to specify the mechanism through which institutions that function in 
exactly the same way can become politicized. Institutions are generally needed to distribute 
financial, material and human resources towards a specific purpose.
00:21:11
For example, the purpose of a university is to impart knowledge and enable research. 
This purpose is also laid down in the corresponding mission statement of such an institution. To 

distribute resources, you also need an administrative apparatus with an administration. And finally, 
there is also a level of actors at which there are scientists, students, staff and artists, to whom these 
available resources are distributed for a specific purpose. Particularly in art scenes and at 
universities, you will meet political activists who want to influence or change institutions for 
ideological reasons.
00:21:58
In a liberal democracy, this is your right and I do not want to deny you this right at 
all. Normally, however, institutions treat the political beliefs of their members and employees as a 
private matter and only respond to political demands if they do not undermine the institution's 
intrinsic values. From time to time, however, this institutional logic can be overridden. This was the 
case, for example, with the student protests in 1968. And that is the case today, as can be seen 
exemplarily at American elite universities.
00:22:40
The reason for this is group polarization. Psychology has shown in empirical studies 
that members of a group take more extreme positions after a discussion than before a discussion. 
However, for such group polarization to occur, a specific condition must be met, namely that these 
are homogeneous value environments. In homogeneous value environments, almost all members of 
a group share the same political, moral, religious or aesthetic values. This is certainly the case in 
universities, where the overwhelming number of students and faculty hold progressive values, 
which correlates with education.
00:23:30
In the United States, there are entire departments in the humanities where there are 
no longer any Republicans or where the ratio of Democrats to Republicans is 20:1 or 70:1. 
However, even if all members of the group share a certain value, there will be a wide range of 
values within the group itself, ranging from moderate positions to radical positions. The law of 
group polarization now states that the radical positions will prevail over the moderate positions in a 
discussion. This means that the group opinion will shift towards the radical values pole in relation 
to the shared value.
00:24:18
The mechanism that underlies and triggers polarization in such groups is the 
expected damage to the reputation of those who are in this group. Anyone who contradicts the 
radical positions seems to represent the value of the group only half-heartedly or only in appearance
and is therefore quickly marginalized or even excluded from the group. The opposing voices will 
quickly fall silent, the spectrum of opinions will narrow and move towards the radical voices that 
seem to represent the group values most purely.
00:25:00
Group polarization can take place in left and right, progressive and conservative 
milieus. While left-wing group polarization occurs primarily in progressive milieus such as 
universities and art scenes, right-wing group polarization is dominant in conservative value milieus,
such as those to be expected in police schools or the army, even though there are always political 
activists in all cultural and educational institutions As we have found and continue to find, the 
phenomenon of cancel culture only became virulent in the last ten years or so.
00:25:40
Political group polarization processes, which can start at any time, were blocked until
then. The administration had little trouble asserting the intrinsic values of its institutions, such as the
search for truth or free artistic expression, in the face of political demands. But this is exactly no 
longer the case today. This unblocking of group polarization processes can be explained as an 
unintended side effect of the digital revolution. In an analog media world, administrations could 
negotiate or moderate many political conflicts in their institutions in secret or simply ignore them.
00:26:27
In the new digital media they lose precisely this latency protection, which leads to 
forced group polarization. On the one hand, the institutions become transparent for political 

observers. Today you have to expect that every sentence, every gesture, every joke and every 
document will sooner or later be photographed or recorded. And on the other hand, institutions are 
becoming much more permeable to political communication. Insofar as such information can then 
be politically framed and communicated back to them via the new media.
00:27:08
Instead of being able to think about and decide on the appropriateness of a political 
demand, the administrators themselves are now drawn into the corresponding political controversies
and thus come under political and moral pressure. This is how feedback loops of political 
communication arise today, as a result of which group polarization processes can reinforce and 
escalate themselves. An immediate consequence of this is that the administrative level is now taking
on activist positions itself.
00:27:44
In many decisions, scientific or artistic decision-making criteria are overwritten or 
replaced by political criteria. What is now setting in is a digitally enforced group polarization that 
did not exist in the analogue media world. More and more political criteria are now being 
incorporated into the institutions' mission statements and also communicated internally and 
externally. In addition, more jobs are being created in institutions with a clear political objective. 
And finally, more and more positions are being filled not based on technical aspects, but rather on 
political grounds.
00:28:27
The deeper reason why these politicization processes are not perceived as such and 
are only very rarely questioned lies in precisely those homogeneous value environments. What 
should you have against values such as diversity, equality and inclusion if you yourself hold liberal 
and progressive views? In the end, no one can escape this logic of politicization and will have to 
make political statements in research proposals, in application letters and in public discussions. A 
political language game becomes obligatory in the institutions, which links core political concepts 
in a very specific way and which, like any ideology, claims to be indisputable.
00:29:19
However, in a politicized institution you can no longer decide between different 
ideologies as you would in a choice; instead, if you do not want to gamble away or lose future 
opportunities, you are forced into this political language game. The logical core of all cancel culture
is that one can no longer simply deny the meaning of political terms under these circumstances. 
Strictly speaking, communication here is not at all in the medium of politics, but in the medium of 
power, which actually contradicts the spirit of liberal democracies.
00:30:02
Such forced political communication also comes into practical conflict with freedom 
of teaching, freedom of science or freedom of art. Institutions that are affected by such forced 
polarization are less and less able to orientate themselves towards their own values and begin to 
produce ideologies instead of art, instead of knowledge and instead of education. In a word, they are
turning into ideology machines. Universities, theaters and museums are very old institutions. They 
emerged in a writing and printing culture.
00:30:41
And they are extremely ill-prepared for today's digital communication. Such 
institutions become dysfunctional and are then increasingly unable to fulfill the function for which 
they were originally created. Since today's forced group polarization is based on a media change 
whose scope can only be compared with the invention of the printing press, the phenomenon of 
cancel culture is not a trivial matter, but a really serious problem. Liberal democracies are genuinely
dependent on a functional differentiation of their communication, so that a loss of boundaries in 
political communication also threatens them at their core.
00:31:31
Science is a communication medium that increases the probability of being able to 
dispute a statement, so that the undeniable language games of any ideology can also be questioned 

through scientific analysis. Education can only be acquired and imparted when students learn to 
interpret phenomena in all their complexity, complexity and ambiguity. And art works with the 
means of ambivalence, hyperaffirmation and irony and, last but not least, aims to ridicule the 
dogmatic language games of politics.
00:32:13
Science, education and art are by their nature anti-ideological and are simply not 
compatible with the undeniable language games of ideologies. That is why they are also protected 
by the constitutional articles of freedom of science, freedom of teaching and freedom of art or, 
better said, the liberal democracies protect themselves with these constitutional principles. If this 
diagnosis is correct, the question naturally arises as to whether and how such institutions are in... 
restabilized in a digital media world.
00:32:52
I made some suggestions for systemic therapy based on the same idea in the last 
chapter of the book. The thesis is that you have to build “ideology interrupters” into the institutions. 
This means that you have to interrupt the feedback loops and resonance amplifiers through which 
political communication in institutions today can reinforce and build itself up. This can be done at 
all three institutional levels: at the level of the mission statements, at the level of the administration 
and at the level of the actors.
00:33:32
You don't have to rely on pure thought experiments here, but you can take a cue from
developments that can already be observed in America and Great Britain today. In 2015, the 
University of Chicago published its so-called Chicago Principles, in which academic freedom was 
once again explicitly integrated into the university's mission statement. This is what it says 
verbatim, and I quote: "The University is committed to the principle that debate or reflection shall 
not be suppressed simply because the ideas presented are viewed by some or even most members of
the University community as offensive, unwise, immoral or unreasonable become." 110 American 
universities have now signed the Chicago Principles and integrated them into their mission 
statements.
00:34:38
And companies like Netflix have also issued such guidelines to combat cancel 
culture in their industries, because you simply cannot produce successful films under these 
conditions. At the administrative level, it is primarily free speech organizations like FIRE that can at
least dampen the feedback loops of political communication. If students or professors or employees 
lose their jobs or are disinvited or are not promoted, these organizations sue the institutions in 
question and thereby force the administration to actually enforce the constitutionally guaranteed 
civil liberties.
00:35:27
Some companies like Coinbase even go so far as to require their employees to be 
politically neutral and ban political controversy in the workplace entirely. This example is 
particularly revealing in our context, as it is completely unsuitable for universities and the art scene.
You cannot simply regulate political activism or political art. This would itself be a censorship 
measure that is incompatible with academic freedom or artistic freedom. In this respect, anti-
Semitism clauses or codes of conduct, such as those recently passed in Kassel and Berlin, are not 
sustainable solutions, but rather to cancel another form of illiberalism, political artists and activist 
scientists themselves.
00:36:26
But what you can do and, in my opinion, must do is to interrupt the self-reinforcing 
processes of political communication. In the book I also discuss other activities that are suitable as 
ideology interrupters. If all of these measures and counterforces work together as a whole, then I am
optimistic that liberal institutions will also be able to be stabilized in a digital media world. And 
that's actually where I've come to the end. Given the general blurring of boundaries in political 
communication, one or the other probably asks the existential question “To be or not to be political”

from time to time.
00:37:13
While writing the book, I often asked myself how I could ensure that the theory of 
ideology machines was not itself perceived and interpreted as an ideology. If there is such suspicion
of ideology, then no science can help. Here one can only argue self-referentially. In other words, I 
can at most apply the ideology criteria I have developed myself to my theory of ideology machines 
and see whether it acts ideologically or non-ideologically under these criteria. I would say that the 
book is non-ideological in that it does not combine political ideas into a consistent conceptual 
context and does not specifically prioritize political ideas.
00:38:04
However, one objection could be that the ideological machines themselves make a 
normative statement: the institutions of science, art and education should not be politicized. The 
theory undoubtedly has this form of residual normativity, insofar as it repeatedly refers to the 
constitution and constitution of liberal democracies in the sense of a final justification authority. But
this is not a political statement. The term liberalism used here does not justify this or that ideology, 
but rather defines the space in which left, right, green, liberal and even populist ideologies can 
compete with one another in illiberal democracies.
00:38:57
And at the very end, a personal comment that might also be informative for you. I 
feel like I'm in the wrong movie every time I carelessly wander into a political discussion. If you 
feel the same way, I can reassure you here. It is an incurable occupational disease. As a humanities 
scientist, you are trained to see the different and contradictory meanings of words and concepts. 
And this ability cannot simply be turned off when it comes to political ideas. Although ideologies 
fulfill an indispensable function in liberal democracies, from the idiosyncratic perspective of 
philosophy it seems to me completely pointless to try to define core political concepts with a 
specific meaning and to argue with someone about the only correct meaning of a political idea.
00:39:56
And with this in mind, I am now looking forward to a non-political discussion. 
Thank you!
What istgood art?
00:00:00
Good morning and many thanks to Maria Spychiger for the wonderful invitation to 
the lecture series. I am delighted to be back here. I talked about kitsch here a few years ago. I think 
the question "What is good music?" is so interesting that I would actually like to expand on it, 
because it concerns the arts in and of themselves. And that's why today I would like to formulate 
"What is good art?" in a more general way, with two question marks. The second question mark is 
intended to indicate a certain doubt as to whether the question of good
00:00:42
art can still be posed so directly today, i.e. whether it can be posed without additional
theoretical differentiations. For reasons that I will discuss in a moment, the status of art today can 
no longer be determined simply by the characteristics or quality features of artworks, but 
contemporary art can be judged solely on the basis of the art communication that a work of art is 
capable of releasing. Accordingly, I will try to answer the question of good art with a 
communication theory approach.
00:01:21
With this in mind, I would like to start by presenting a general concept of 
communication. In a second step, I will then specify this in terms of art communication in order to 
be able to argue that self-reflective art communication can also be understood as "good art" in a 
normative sense. So much in advance. The sociologist Niklas Luhmann developed a tripartite 

concept of communication, according to which all communication consists of a threefold selection, 
namely a selection of information, of
00:02:02
communication and of understanding. And that means that a threefold contingency 
comes into play. The model therefore draws attention from the outset to the improbability that 
communication can take place successfully at all. In addition, there are two abstract communication 
partners in this model, which Luhmann refers to as "ego" and "alter". Let's take an example. One 
person says to another: "The sun is shining." The first selection means that this is an observation 
relevant to communication and all other everyday perceptions are irrelevant for this
00:02:49
communication. In addition, a selection is also made with regard to the way in which 
this information is communicated. You can make this statement casually, or you can make it with an
ironic undertone or with a certain astonishment in your voice. In our case, the sentence "The sun is 
shining!" should be exclaimed in surprise. Finally, the information must also be understood due to 
its communicative character, which makes a third selection necessary. Understanding therefore 
means interpreting the distinction between information and communication
00:03:32
as a unit. Let's say a couple is on vacation by the sea. And the woman's message "The
sun is shining" is understood by the man as an invitation to go swimming. However, since such 
communication consists of selection, misunderstandings are inevitable. If the two of them talked in 
the morning about the weather being bad and that they can't go to the beach but have to go shopping
and take an umbrella with them, then there are two different contexts of understanding: going 
swimming and going shopping.
00:04:13
If the man selects context two, then he could understand the message "The sun is 
shining" as a statement from his wife that you don't need to take an umbrella with you when you go 
shopping. You can see that everyday communication also consists of selection and that 
misunderstandings can occur at any time. Although Luhmann wrote a great sociology of art with 
"The Art of Society", this book, like almost all of his books on systems theory, remains very 
abstract and largely dispenses with examples,
00:04:49
which is somewhat problematic when it comes to art. Luhmann never explains in 
detail, i.e. using works of art, how art communication should actually work. Like all 
communication, art communication consists of a threefold selection of information, communication 
and understanding. What changes compared to everyday communication, however, is that the artist 
disappears behind his work. The artist becomes the gray shadow of his work, which communicates 
independently of him once it is in the world. Art communicates, as Luhmann puts it, by means of 
works of art.
00:05:34
My initial thesis is that the three basic components of art communication, i.e. 
information, communication and understanding, can be concretized with the help of the art-specific 
categories of medium, work and concept. We are therefore dealing here with a special case of 
Luhmann's concept of communication. Every work of art has an information component, as it 
provides the recipient with specific background knowledge a priori, i.e. before any experience of 
art. In art communication, this source of information takes the form of concepts, which are then 
made
00:06:23
explicit in conceptual art. On the one hand, art concepts include explicit linguistic 
descriptions, such as those provided by a picture title. And on the other hand, the conceptual core of
a work of art also includes everything that I can immediately and directly perceive in this work of 
art and that is self-evident for every viewer. In the original sense of the Italian word concetto, the 
concepts also include the thematic idea, for example the idea that a sculpture is a crucifixion scene. 

In short, the concept includes everything that can be perceived and experienced in a work of art
00:07:11
as directly as in the living world, or what is explicitly attributed to it by language. In 
art, messages are produced through specific artistic media. Such art media can be art styles or isms 
or aesthetics. The media of the arts endow conceptual information with a performative content 
(gehalt) that pictures, sculptures, buildings or poems do not possess as mere information carriers. 
Finally, there are the works of art with which art communicates. The work of art synthesizes in itself
both the self-evident information and the media-specific
00:07:57
messages, and this difference between information and message stored in the work 
can then also be understood like any communication. Luhmann once described art ad hoc as 
compact communication. The term conveys very nicely that the combination of forms in a work of 
art already contains the communication that is only then released in the reception. In other words, 
works of art store communication potential in a very condensed form. In art communication, too, a 
threefold selection of information, of communication and of
00:08:40
understanding takes place, but in this case via concepts, via media and via works of 
art. This selection can be more or less strong. In the traditional fine arts, they are hardly noticeable 
because art at that time was embedded in a cultural context of understanding that was equally 
binding for everyone. Michelangelo's crucifix of Santa Spirito was embedded in a religious context,
i.e. in a cultural context in which the concept of Jesus Christ on the cross was not in need of 
explanation. The novelty of this early work by Michelangelo was the specific Renaissance
00:09:24
aesthetic with which this religious symbol was expressed here. The artist had taken 
an idealized, beautiful boy as his model and depicted the figure of Christ without a linen loincloth. 
The selection of this representation, i.e. the medium, was closely interwoven with the zeitgeist of 
the time. The ideals of beauty of antiquity and the humanistic ideals derived from them were taken 
as a guide. Accordingly, it was no problem for the intellectual elite to understand the work as an 
expression of their own new age.
00:10:06
The missing loincloth is also not an artistic selection that completely falls out of the 
context of meaning of the work or contradicts it, but rather takes the idea of the Renaissance man 
who recognizes himself in Christ to an extreme. The crucifix of Santo Spirito thus unleashes a 
communication of art that part of the population of Florence has accepted and part has rejected. But 
despite its innovative character, the work was self-explanatory for everyone. This kind of self-
evident art communication was prevalent throughout the entire period of the fine
00:10:48
arts. The medium of the arts structured every aesthetic experience in advance 
towards an experience of beauty. In the visual arts, the medium of fine art is constituted by rules of 
proportion, by symmetries, by golden sections and by central perspective. In music, it is the 
counterpoint, the time signature and the tonal system that prepare every composition for a beautiful 
harmonic sound. In the fine arts, medium, work and concept were linked by a shared cultural 
knowledge and were therefore not perceived as separate components.
00:11:32
In the diagram, the dotted line between medium, work and concept stands for this 
cultural coupling of the three components of art communication. In principle, this scheme can be 
applied to all pre-modern art forms, be it medieval, ancient or even prehistoric art. All this changed 
with the advent of aesthetic modernism at the beginning of the 20th century. Art had developed its 
own autonomous communication system in which the arts became free to actively negate the 
traditional concept of art with the help of new works of art.
00:12:14
First of all, this concerned the general claim of all fine arts to be beautiful. The 

formation of modern isms such as Cubism or Expressionism thus led to a negation of the medium of
art, which until then had prepared the aesthetic experience of beauty, as indicated here by the loss of
segment M, the medium. Instead of beauty, Germaine Richier's Christ of Assisi now focuses on an 
alternative aesthetic value, namely the aesthetic value of the event, which in turn forms the basis of 
Expressionism.
00:12:58
However, the components of the work and the concept, i.e. W and K, are still closely 
connected, i.e. they are naturally linked. The concept says that it is a Christ figure and the sculpture 
shows a Christ on the cross. In this particular work, however, this linkage is expressed in a 
particularly striking way, in that the figure in the back is fused with the cross. The effects of the 
negation of the fine arts medium on art communication are serious. There is now a bifurcation 
between insiders and outsiders that did not exist in the past.
00:13:46
The majority of art viewers could no longer understand the early works of 
Expressionism as art at all. For them, the negation of beauty is simply not art communication. For a 
small section of art insiders, on the other hand, the negation of the medium itself had become a 
message that these works were modern and progressive. With Marshall McLuhan, one would have 
to say,  ironically: The negation of the medium is the message. As soon as art has developed an 
autonomous communication system, it falls into a
00:14:27
logic of surpassing, through which new art is generated via a negation of art that is 
old, that already exists. In the historical avant-garde, this inevitably leads to the abandonment of the
work character of art. Works of art are not simply combinations of forms, as Luhmann puts it, but 
the decisive factor in a work of art, at least in the traditional sense, is that its perceptible forms come
together to form a complex of meaning. It is precisely this premise that is negated in Joseph Beuys' 
Crucifixion.
00:15:04
The work is reduced to the information in the title, i.e. the concept that this 
assemblage is to be received as a crucifixion. The widespread interpretation that the two bottles on 
the wooden blocks refer to Mary and John and that the wooden slat symbolizes Jesus on the cross 
are statements that can only be made with the help of insider information. Generally speaking, this 
type of art can be described as conceptual art. The only thing that can be understood about these 
works is the concept that this is art in which the
00:15:44
work category and the media category are deliberately annulled at the same time. As 
incomprehensible as conceptual art was at first, at some point such works also became a natural 
manifestation of art in the art system. A zero point has thus been reached in the logic of surpassing 
in the history of aesthetic modernity. The conceptual nature of art can not be negated. One cannot 
simply do without a concept in art communication, because it encodes a crucial minimal piece of 
information, namely that this is art and not social work or education or politics
00:16:31
or science. All of these themes, all of these topics are quite virulent in contemporary 
art. But observers must still be able to distinguish the game of art from the seriousness of life. 
Nevertheless, conceptual art has not yet exhausted the possibilities of negation. Once the 
expectation has been established in the art system that avant-garde art can dispense with the use of 
media and the work character of art, then one is also free to negate these very expectations once 
again. It is precisely this option that aesthetic postmodernism has discovered, insofar as it
00:17:16
carries out a negation of the negation of the medium of fine art. There is now a 
systematic re-inclusion of figurative painting, figurative sculpture, tonal music, ornament or rhyme 
in the advanced art scenes, i.e. all those forms that were once negated and tabooed by the avant-
gardes. Now Kippenberger can also use the clear, identifiable figure of a cross and a crucified man 

again, albeit in the form of a green frog. Both the subject and the garish aesthetics of this exemplary
postmodern work fulfill a very
00:18:00
specific function in the communication system of art. They block the interpretation 
that Kippenberger, by disregarding the taboos of the avant-garde, is simply a conservative who 
creates nothing new. The absurd-sounding title "First the feet" also fulfills such a distancing 
function. The the frog of consumption had probably asked to be nailed by the feet first so that he 
could still eat his egg and drink his beer. Kippenberger thus reuses the medium of figurative art not 
simply naively, but with irony.
00:18:41
This ironic distance is represented here in the scheme by the gap between concept 
and medium. It is characteristic of aesthetic postmodernism that the category of work remains 
neutralized, although one could of course speak colloquially of a work of art here as well. 
Terminologically precise, however, works of art are not simply arrangements of forms or 
combinations of forms, but also create contexts of meaning, which is clearly not the case here or is 
being torpedoed. Kippenberger deliberately blocks a synthesis of meaning when he combines a 
wooden cross, a green
00:19:31
frog, a beer glass and an egg into an object of art communication. Kippenberger's 
crucifix cannot be understood as a meaningful interpretation of the Christian faith; at best, it negates
Christianity in and of itself. We are dealing here with a specifically postmodern form of art 
communication that exhibits a double coding. The outsiders of the art system can only understand 
the crucified frog as a maximum affront to religion. Insiders, on the other hand, see the frog on the 
cross as an expression of irony, which fulfils the
00:20:09
function of allowing Kippenberger to work figuratively and representationally again 
without being marginalized as a reactionary artist in the art system. Of course, postmodern artists 
have been labeled reactionaries. But sooner or later, this judgment fell back on the humorless know-
it-alls who simply didn't get the joke of postmodernism. Finally, Maurizio Cattelan's "La Nona Ora"
shows that even the postmodern play with art styles, aesthetics, motifs and themes has not remained
the last word in modern art history.
00:20:52
Here, too, there is a whole series of elements such as shards of glass, a lump of rock, 
the Pope John Paul II, a red carpet and the title "In the ninth hour". At first glance, all of this seems 
to have little to do with each other, but it can set a complex process of interpretation in motion. The 
work character of art also returns here in the form of a double negation, i.e. a negation of the 
artwork negated by the avant-garde. The shards of glass on the floor indicate that the rock came 
from above and that the Pope was hit
00:21:31
by a meteorite, which is not written anywhere. The "ninth hour" in turn refers to a 
biblical passage in which Jesus cries out on the cross "My God, my God, why have you forsaken 
me?" The probability that a meteorite could actually hit the Pope is zero by earthly standards, so 
that divine intervention would actually be necessary. Strictly speaking, the artwork communicates 
that God exists by symbolically slaying the Pope. Unlike Kippenberger's crucified frog, which 
makes a nonsensical statement, Cattelan's slain pope
00:22:14
generates a completely surprising aesthetic idea. The recurrence of the work-
character makes understanding this type of art difficult and extremely time-consuming, because the 
selection effort of such art communication increases enormously. Neither the concept nor the title 
says what the work means, nor does a medium preform the work. Accordingly, there are now 
additional open spaces between work and medium and between work and concept, which in turn are
indicated in the schema by these gaps between W and M and between W

00:22:55
and K. Cattelan's work can again be read as a complex of meaning, but this requires a
much, much greater interpretative effort than Michelangelo's Crucifix of Santo Spirito. In 
Michelangelo's time, work, medium and concept were linked in an overarching cultural context, 
whereas Cattelan's "In the Ninth Hour" is a singular artwork that only comes into being in the 
artistic communication between the work and its viewer - regardless of what the artist may actually 
have had in mind. In this case, I would like to speak of an art that reflects on itself or, in short, of 
reflexive
00:23:42
art. Medium, work and concept are decoupled here and must and can therefore be 
freely coupled with each other again. For art communication in general, this means that the 
selection possibilities for information, for communication and for understanding are increasing and 
therefore the interpretation and understanding of such works is also becoming extremely 
complicated. Here you can see a so-called historical series for the sculpture, which I have now 
narrowed down thematically to crucifixions. The strategy behind this is that such series abstract her 
from everything that is not important
00:24:28
in the history of modern art. This provides a visual argument that can be used to 
immediately read when the medium and the work are dropped out in the history of modern art and 
when they are later step  by step included again. If one analyzes the history of the arts as a history of
art communication, then the great historical caesuras of modern art can be explained as a sequence 
of simple and double negations. The progress of modernism therefore did not consist in ever greater
abstraction or in
00:25:08
overcoming the work-character of art or in a complete dissolution of the boundaries 
of the notion of art, but rather in an increase in the degree of freedom of art communication. My 
ambition is to soon publish this theoretical model of aesthetic modernity in the form of such an atlas
of images, composed of historical series in respect  to paintings, sculptures, architecture, poetry and 
music. Ultimately, this theoretical model is model  overarching different genres, because every type 
of art communication is dependent on an information, a communication and an understanding
00:25:55
component, which is why such historical series can be constructed equally in 
different genres. The history of modern art can thus be reconstructed across genres as a history of 
the differentiation of art communication. This leads to a paradigm shift, namely a paradigm shift 
from a material aesthetic to a content aesthetic, which runs through conceptual art. Until the 
historical avant-garde, "novelty" in modern art was always defined by the new aesthetic material. 
However, conceptual art started a trend in the opposite direction because the scope for
00:26:41
innovation in material aesthetics had been exhausted. And the novelty of art is then 
increasingly generated through new aesthetic content (gehalt). This brings me to the actual core of 
my presentation, namely the normative question "What is good art?" with two question marks. The 
first question mark means that the normative question of good art cannot be avoided; the second 
question mark calls the question itself into question, because it can no longer really be asked today. 
A far-reaching distinction can be derived from the theoretical model of aesthetic modernity,
00:27:25
namely the distinction between self-evident and self-reflective art communication. 
First of all, this is a purely descriptive distinction that arises from the intrinsic history of the 
differentiation of the arts in the 20th century can be derived. The differentiation between work, 
medium and concept increases the degree of freedom to create a difference between information 
and communication, making the understanding of works of art ever more challenging. In a self-
evident art communication, the selection effort remains very low, so that one

00:28:06
usually does not even notice that three selections are being made here. You need 
much less prior knowledge and you also need much less time, because these three selections are 
always pre-selected by a shared cultural context. Accordingly, there are only a few possible 
combinations, in order to combine possible information with possible messages and then to 
understand these possible combinations as an artistic communication. The question now is whether 
this distinction between self-evident and self-reflective art
00:28:47
communication can also be used as a normative distinction. Could it not be said that 
the historical achievements of aesthetic modernity themselves become normative for a modern 
society? Would it not be possible to characterize self-reflective art communication as "good" in a 
specific sense? I would like to answer the question in the same way. However, this creates a 
fundamental difficulty with the negative value, i.e. on the side of self-evident art communication. 
The claim would be that all self-evident art communication - and this applies to at least
00:29:36
99.99% of all art - is "bad" in and of itself. Ultimately, every popular culture must be 
based on a self-evident art communication, otherwise it cannot be popular. In the 21st century, 
however, we can no longer simply say, as Adorno once did, that all popular art forms are inferior. In
addition, postmodern art has included the "lower" art forms in "high" culture art, so that the 
distinction between popular culture and high culture has become completely useless as a normative 
distinction. What is possible, however, is to distinguish between two different spheres of value.
00:30:25
With the formation of aesthetic modernism, not only did a reflective art develop, but 
there was also a parallel explosion of popular culture, which is not simply bad art. The result is a  
tremendeous number of popular art forms, including fashion, advertising and design, all of which 
develop their own aesthetic canon of values and judge with their own value criteria, what is 
successful or unsuccessful in their field, i.e. what is "good" or "bad". These forms of self-evident art
communication are based on different cultural contexts, in which
00:31:16
different cultural values are also inscribed. And such cultural values, and this is the 
crucial point, these cultural values can be expressed more or less well by an artwork. Rock n roll, 
for example, can express the feeling of the sexual revolution more or less convincing. Punk can 
sound more or less anarchistic. Political art can articulate a political stance more or less well. A 
fashion can capture the zeitgeist or not. As cultural contexts are no longer integrated by morals and 
religion, as was the case in
00:31:57
Michelangelo's time, popular culture is fragmenting into a multitude of different 
value spheres, which are also becoming increasingly compartmentalized. This is a result of the 
advancing individualization. In the end, there are hundreds of different styles of heavy metal, with 
fans always considering their own style to be the only right one and judging all other styles as 
"wrong" or "bad". When the cultural contexts become very narrow and specific, one no longer 
speaks of popular culture, but of subcultures, which also unfold in their small field in the mode of
00:32:39
self-evident art communication. Modern society thus forms an almost infinite 
number of value spheres in which shared cultural values are constructed by certain groups to 
constitute the standard for "good" and "bad" art. Good art then articulates the values that constitute 
the community of values particularly well. Bad art does not achieve this articulation. Both pre-
modern art and the popular and subcultural art forms of today are based on a self-evident art 
communication and accordingly exhibit a structural affinity: it is equally a
00:33:28
question of representational art. In each case, it is about the representation of ideas 
shared by a group of people. In contrast, reflective art forms a completely different mode of 
communication. The art ideal of reflexive art is the singular work of art that does not presuppose 

shared cultural contexts of understanding, but instead generates its own context of understanding 
anew in its own work. Reflective art does not, of course, simply want to represent cultural ideas, but
in most cases it will do just that.
00:34:14
In the corresponding art scenes, such works are then assigned negative value 
concepts: the works are unorigional or boring or uncool or kitschy, and so on and so forth. 
Reflective art thus follows an ideal of innovation and does not want to be a self-evident art 
communication and creates its own internal value difference with its own positive value and its own
negative value due to its external demarcation from self-evident art. But the question now is: 
According to which criterion can singular artworks actually be judged
00:34:59
and evaluated? They do not refer to specific cultural values that certain groups and 
people share. The reference of reflexive art is also not culture, but society. Modern societies are 
characterized by the fact that they evolve and accordingly create consequential problems of their 
own evolution. However, social problems must first of all be perceived as real problems and 
identified as such, which is itself problematic. The self-descriptions, the patterns of experience and 
the ideologies with which society has been
00:35:53
perceived and understood up to now systematically prevent us from seeing and 
describing society as it has actually become. At this point, self-reflective art communication gains 
its social function. Precisely because the singular works of art do not rely on any given cultural 
contexts, or at least do not want to, and neither affirm nor criticize these contexts, they also play 
their part in neutralizing existing cultural contexts. Accordingly, reflexive art is one of the places in 
the pre-political space where society can rewrite
00:36:37
its entrenched self-descriptions and liquefy ideologies that have become dominant. I 
have just published this book "Ideology  Machines. How Cancel culture works". The book describes
how and why there is a strong politicization of institutions in liberal democracies today. This affects
educational institutions, the sciences, but also contemporary art in particular. In politicized art 
scenes, however, reflective art sooner or later disappears, which also means that art in these places 
can no longer actually fulfil its social function, namely to provoke new
00:37:23
self-descriptions of society. What emerges instead is mostly political art that can fall 
back on a shared consensus of values. Accordingly, art communication then takes on the form of 
self-evident art communication again, except that the shared self-understanding is a political one. 
And that brings me to the end. The question "What is good art?" cannot be avoided. Precisely 
because reflexive art is extremely unlikely, it must be privately sponsored or state-subsidized and 
therefore requires legitimation.
00:38:09
At some point, the question must always be answered as to which artists deserve the 
financial and symbolic resources and which do not. And the general answer is always normative: 
the good ones, of course. Thank you very much.
00:00:00
Mit Ihrer 15. Ausgabe geriet die bedeutendste  Kunstausstellung in Deutschland in 
eine schwere Krise. Die Findungskommission für die documenta  16 ist bekanntlich geschlossen 
zurückgetreten, und es scheint schwierig zu sein, eine neue  Findungskommission 
zusammenzustellen. Was aber ist überhaupt das Problem, vor dem die documenta heute  steht? Das 
ist die Frage, mit der ich mich in der nächsten halben Stunde beschäftigen möchte. Ich  werde ein 
Argument in drei Schritten entfalten. Meine Ausgangsthese lautet, dass das Problem der  documenta
kein politisches Problem ist und sich

00:00:39
dementsprechend auch nicht politisch lösen lässt.  Vielmehr besteht es darin und das 
wäre dann auch meine zentrale These, worauf ich auch den Großteil  des Vortrages konzentrieren 
möchte, dass sich die documenta in den letzten Jahren als Institution  stark politisiert hat. Wenn das 
die richtige Problemanalyse ist, dann kann man natürlich auch  Ideen und Vorschläge entwickeln, 
und das ist dann der dritte Punkt, wie man eine documenta 16  realisieren könnte. Antisemitismus 
ist ein politisches Problem, das Problem der Documenta  ist es hingegen nicht. Wäre das der Fall,
00:01:21
dann gäbe es politische Lösungen wie etwa eine  Antisemitismusklausel, wie sie der 
Kultursenator von Berlin vorgeschlagen und dann wieder  zurückgenommen hat. Oder eben ein 
Verhaltenskodex wie ihn jetzt die documenta entwickelt hat. Doch  dieser Kodex sollte dann eben 
doch nicht mehr für die künstlerische Leitung gelten, wodurch das  ganze Unternehmen eigentlich 
gescheitert ist. Zwar lassen sich antisemitische Motive auf dem  Banner People's Justice von Taring 
Padi finden, aber diese Motive sind von der Kunstfreiheit  gedeckt. Und der Grund ist 
offensichtlich:
00:02:02
Im Kontext der Kunst wird jedes Symbol zum  Gegenstand einer Interpretation. Das 
müsste erst recht auf dieses riesige Wimmelbild zutreffen,  auf dem sich die antisemitischen Motive 
eigentlich in Kunst auflösen müssten. Und dennoch haben 25  Personen und Institutionen 
Strafanzeige erstattet. Der Kommentar der Staatsanwaltschaft war, wie zu  erwarten, dass es keinen 
Anfangsverdacht wegen einer verfolgbaren Straftat gäbe. Die Probe  aufs Exempel hatte Jonathan 
Meese bereits vor zehn Jahren gemacht, indem er auf einer  Performance Hitlergruß und 
Hakenkreuz gezeigt
00:02:47
hatte. Obwohl diese Symbole verfassungswidrig  sind und in Deutschland unter 
Strafe stehen, kann man sie natürlich in einer Performance  verwenden. Und Jonathan Meese hat 
sich das per Gerichtsbeschluss auch bescheinigen lassen. Im  Kontext der Kunst verwandeln sich 
auch verbotene politische Symbole in ein Darstellungsmittel  der Kunst. Das Problem der 
documenta 15 ist kein politisches Problem, weil die Politik in  liberalen Demokratien den 
Symbolgebrauch der Kunst prinzipiell nicht regulieren kann. Und wenn  sie anfängt, den 
Symbolgebrauch zu regulieren,
00:03:28
dann befinden wir uns nicht mehr in einer  liberalen, sondern eher in einer illiberalen 
Demokratie. Insofern lässt sich das Problem, wie  gesagt, auch nicht mit politischen Mitteln lösen, 
wie eben mit einer Antisemitismusklausel  oder mit einem Verhaltenskodex. Und dennoch, das ist 
offensichtlich, kann die documenta  nicht so weitermachen wie bisher. Was also ist das Problem, vor
dem die documenta heute  steht? Damit komme ich zu meinem zweiten Punkt. Die documenta hat 
sich als Institution so stark  politisiert, dass sie eigentlich ihr Privileg
00:04:03
der Kunstfreiheit verspielt hat. Sie wird in  der Gesellschaft nur noch als eine 
politische Veranstaltung wahrgenommen. Sie sehen, ich  mache eine Unterscheidung zwischen 
Politik und Politisierung. Bei dieser Politisierung sind  zwei Faktoren entscheidend. Diese zwei 
Faktoren verstärken sich wechselseitig und und ergänzen  sich. Der erste Faktor ist ein interner 
Faktor. Er liegt in der Kunst selbst begründet.  Der zweite Faktor ist ein externer Faktor, der eben 
von außerhalb auf die Kunst einwirkt.  Beide Faktoren zusammen schaukeln sich auf und
00:04:42
führen dann zu jener Resonanzkatastrophe,  die die documenta erlebt hat. Ich komme

zunächst zu diesem internen Faktor.  Dieser besteht meines Erachtens darin, dass die Politisierung 
der documenta eigentlich  aus der immanenten Logik der ästhetischen Moderne folgt. Die Logik der
Moderne besteht  darin, dass sie auf Neuheit setzt und Neuheit über eine Negation des Alten 
generiert,  das heißt über eine Negation von Kunst, die es bereits gibt. Generell führt diese  Logik 
der Moderne in eine Überbietungslogik,
00:05:32
in eine permanente Inklusion von Nichtkunst  in die Kunst, was eben zur Folge hat, 
dass sich das Feld der Kunst in das Feld der Nichtkunst  entgrenzt. Wenn man dieser Logik der 
ästhetischen Moderne kompromisslos folgt, dann gelangt man  zu dem, was ich Documenta-Kunst 
nennen möchte. Die Documenta-Kunst ist ihre Idee nach eine auf  Dauer gestellte Avantgarde. 
Genau deswegen kann sie auch den Anspruch erheben, alle fünf Jahre  den Stand der avanciertesten 
Gegenwartskunst dokumentieren zu können. Die Genese der  Documenta-Kunst aus der Logik der 
ästhetischen
00:06:11
Moderne lässt sich verkürzt in vier Schritten  rekonstruieren, die ich Ihnen kurz 
skizzieren möchte. Die moderne Kunst setzt in dem Moment ein,  in dem sie die Idee, dass Kunst 
notwendigerweise schöne Kunst ist, zurückweist und stattdessen  sich als nicht-mehr-schöne Kunst 
entfaltet. Diese Negation der schönen Künste setzt einen Freiraum  frei zur Exploration von anderen
ästhetischen Werten, nämlich von Werten wie Ereignishaftigkeit,  Ambivalenz und Erhabenheit. 
Diese ästhetischen Werte werden dann an Stelle von Schönheit  exploriert, und man konnte dann 
spezifische
00:06:58
Ismen der klassischen Moderne wie dem Kubismus  und dem Expressionismus 
entdecken. Wenn man dieser Negationslogik einfach folgt kann man auch die  Kunst der klassischen
Moderne als gegebene Kunst voraussetzen und zurückweisen. Man kann also die  Erwartung 
negieren, dass die Kunst ästhetische Kunst ist. Dies hat zur historischen Avantgarde  geführt, die 
den Werkbegriff und den ästhetischen Charakter der Kunst negiert. Diese Idee hatte  zuerst 
Duchamp, aber sie kulminiert schließlich in dem, was man analytische Konzeptkunst nennt.  Diese 
Idee von Materialfortschritt hatte sich
00:07:47
aber bereits vor einem halben Jahrhundert  erschöpft. Es gab dann so letzte Bilder 
und letzte Kompositionen wie die ausradierte Zeichnung  von de Kooning oder 4'33'' von John 
Cage. Damit steht die Kunst vor einer Alternative: Sie  kann ihren Neuheitsanspruch entweder 
preisgeben, dann wird sie postmodern, oder sie kann diesen  Neuheitsanspruch reformulieren, das 
heißt, sie kann den Begriff des Neuen neu definieren.  Das ist die Option, die mehr oder weniger die
documenta auch gefolgt ist. Das heißt man  generiert Neuheit nicht mehr über die Exploration
00:08:30
von neuem ästhetischen Material, sondern  über neue Gehalte, sprich über neue 
Inhalte und Themen. Hier ist die Kunst eigentlich nicht  begrenzt, denn das Themenreservoir ist in 
einer evolvierenden Gesellschaft unerschöpflich. An  diesem Bifurkationspunkt wird dann auch die 
Unterscheidung zwischen Documenta-Kunst  und Kunstmarkt-Kunst merklich. Die die Kunstmarkt-
Kunst entfaltet sich als postmoderne  Kunst, weil diese Kunst, wieder auf Ästhetik setzt. Die 
postmoderne Kunst zeigt sich an der  Wiederverwendung von ästhetischen Material und
00:09:14
von traditionellen Stilen. Eine solche Kunst  kann sich natürlich auf dem Kunstmarkt
besser behaupten. Die Documenta-Kunst hingegen  verharrt bei einer konzeptbasierten, 
anästhetischen und sich selbst entgrenzenden  Kunst. Das ist, grob gesagt, die Idee von Kunst, der 

die documenta folgt. Dass diese  Unterscheidung tatsächlich existiert, zeigt sich sehr schön daran, 
dass einer der bedeutendsten  Künstler nie auf die documenta eingeladen wurde, nämlich Jeff 
Koons. Die Überbietungslogik der  Moderne hat sich damit aber noch nicht erschöpft.
00:09:56
Was ich nämlich ändert, ist die Bedeutung von dem,  was Nichtkunst ist. Vorher 
bezog die Nichtkunst auf Charakteristiken der Kunst, also auf das  ästhetische Material, auf den 
Werkbegriff, auf Gattungsgrenzen und auf Genre usw. Jetzt  bezieht sich die Kunst aber direkt auf 
das Leben. Die Avantgarde sucht die Einheit von Kunst und  Leben bzw. die Überschreitung der 
Grenze zwischen Kunst und Leben. Eigentlich geht es dieser  Avantgarde oder der Documenta-
Kunst aber gar nicht um alle Aspekte, um alle Aspekte des Lebens  gleichermaßen, sondern um eine
Lebenswirklichkeit
00:10:42
mit Fortschritts- und Kritikpotential.  Konkret geht es dann um Kapitalismuskritik, 
Klimaschutz, Postkolonialismus,  Feminismus, nachhaltige Städteplanung, utopische 
Lebensformen, um soziale Arbeit,  um soziale Bewegung und sogar um das Kochen. Die Inklusion 
von Nichtkunst in die Kunst  enthält also Vorschläge der Umgestaltung des gesellschaftlichen 
Lebens. Solche Vorschläge  können angenommen und abgelehnt werden bzw. Sie werden von 
Teilen der Bevölkerung angenommen  und von Teilen der Bevölkerung abgelehnt. Und
00:11:16
das heißt, die Document-Kunst ist genuin  politisch. Die documenta inkludiert in der 
Gefolgschaft der historischen Avantgarde nicht  willkürlich das Leben, sondern sie verbündet sich 
quasi mit der einen oder anderen Form von  politischem Aktivismus. Entsprechend liegt der 
Documenta-Kunst auch ein spezifischer Kunstbegriff  zugrunde, nach dem die Kunst immer schon 
politisch verfasst sei. So sagt etwa Adam Szymczyk, der  Kurator der vorletzten documenta 14: 
"Unsere Ausstellung hat zwar kein politisches Thema,  aber man könnte sagen, dass sie politisch 
denkt.
00:11:57
Hinter praktisch jeder Entscheidung,  die wir im Vorfeld getroffen haben, steckt 
politisches Denken. Und auch die  Auswahl der Künstler ist mit wenigen Ausnahmen politisch 
begründet." Die documenta 15  lässt sich also als ein Kulminationspunkt einer Kunstkonzeption 
verstehen, die der Logik  der Avantgarde radikal folgt und versucht, die Avantgarde auf Dauer zu 
stellen. Auf der einen  Seite hält sie an einem spröden, konzeptbasierten, anästhetischen 
Avantgarde-Stil fest, und auf  der anderen Seite wird die Fortschrittlichkeit,
00:12:33
die man mit künstlerischen Mitteln eigentlich  nicht mehr erreichen kann, durch die 
Anbindung an progressive politische Bewegungen garantiert.  Vielleicht kann man es später einmal 
auch als ein Verdienst der documenta 15 werten, dass sie  dieser Logik der Grenzüberschreitung 
vollständig ausgeschöpft hat und auch vollständig erschöpft  hat und damit eben an einem 
Nullpunkt angelangt ist. Für das Publikum jedenfalls war die  Unterscheidbarkeit von Kunst und 
Nichtkunst bzw. von Kunst und Politik kaum noch wahrzunehmen.  Diese Idee, dass Kunst immer 
schon politisch sei,
00:13:12
wurzelt tief im Selbstverständnis der historischen  Avantgarde. Der Anspruch auf 
künstlerischen Fortschritt war immer zugleich, oder zumindest  sehr oft, an den Anspruch 
gekoppelt, dass die Kunst im Dienste des gesellschaftlichen  Fortschritts steht. Es gibt aber einen 
großen Unterschied zwischen dem Kunstverständnis  der historischen Avantgarde und der 
engagierten Kunst der letzten beiden Dokumenten. Bei einem  Künstler wie Joseph Beuys war der 

politische Anspruch in einer komplizierten Künstlerästhetik  der sozialen Plastik verankert und 
eingebettet,
00:13:49
aus der sich unmittelbar keine politischen  Handlungsanweisungen ableiten lassen. 
Die politische Kunst von heute macht  hingegen unmissverständlich politische Ansagen, da sie nicht
in Privatmythologien  und in Künstler-Ästhetiken begründet ist, sondern den politischen Ideologien 
aktivistischer Bewegungen folgt, mit denen sie verbündet ist. Politischer Kunst  und politischer 
Aktivismus sind also in vielen Fallen identisch. Es gab zu Zeiten von Beuys  noch eine Grenze 
zwischen Kunst und Politik,
00:14:29
die man wahrnehmbar und erfahrbar überschreiten  konnte, wohingegen heute sich 
der Unterschied von Kunst und Politik überhaupt nicht mehr  wahrnehmen lässt. Es kommt, wie 
gesagt, aber noch ein externer Faktor hinzu, der die documenta  erst in diese existenzielle Krise 
geführt hat. Es dürfte kein Zufall gewesen sein, dass die letzte  documenta, die dem Primat der 
Kunst gefolgt ist, die documenta zwölf war von Buergel und Noack aus  dem Jahr 2007. Es ist 
nämlich das gleiche Jahr, in dem auch das erste iPhone auf den  Markt kam und die Digitalisierung 
mit
00:15:07
ihren Netzwerkeffekten und mit ihren sozialen  Medien in den Kunstszenen relevant 
wurde. Dieser externe Faktor der Politisierung betrifft  nicht nur die documenta oder die Künste, 
sondern diese Politisierung erfasst jetzt sehr  viele Institutionen in liberalen Demokratien, also etwa 
Universitäten oder Theater oder  auch Unternehmen. Ich habe eine entsprechende Theorie zu dieser 
Form der Politisierung in  dem Buch "Ideologiemaschinen" entwickelt. Ich werde dieses Argument  
jetzt nur kurz skizzieren,
00:15:52
weil ich mich gern auf den documenta-Aspekt  konzentrieren möchte. Aber wenn 
Bedarf besteht, können wir das ja in der Diskussion  dann noch ein bisschen näher erörtern. Die 
zentrale These dieses Buches lautet,  dass der digitale Medienwandel zu einer allgemeinen 
Politisierung von Institutionen  führt. Wenn man diese These vertritt, braucht man natürlich ein 
Minimalmodell von dem,  was eine Institution eigentlich ist. Institutionen werden dafür geschaffen, 
um für einen bestimmten  Zweck Ressourcen zu verteilen, also finanzielle
00:16:28
Ressourcen oder personelle Ressourcen. Hierfür  braucht man ein Leitbild, in dem 
dieser Zweck festgeschrieben wird. Universitäten zum Beispiel  sollen Bildung generieren und 
Bildung vermitteln. Und in Bezug auf diesen Zweck werden dann  diese Ressourcen von einer 
Administration oder von einem Verwaltungsapparat verteilt.  Der digitale Medienwandel bedeutet 
für diesen Verwaltungsapparat einerseits, dass er von  außen beobachtbar wird. Damit verliert die 
Administration ihren Latenzschutz, den  sie in einer analogen Medienwelt besaß.
00:17:17
Zudem entstehen durch die sozialen Medien eine  Vielzahl von Möglichkeiten von 
außen in diese Institution hineinzukommunizieren. Das hat zur  Folge, dass die Administration sich 
politisch und moralisch unter Druck setzen lässt, und zwar  unter einem Druck, den es vordem nicht
gab. Die Institutionen beginnen sich dann allmählich  nicht mehr an ihren Eigenwerten zu 
orientieren, sondern sie beginnen, sich an politischen Werten  auszurichten. Es bilden sich in den 
Institutionen Feedbackschleifen von politischer Kommunikation  aus, die 
Gruppenpolarisierungsprozesse auslösen

00:18:00
und – an und für sich – in den Institutionen  politische Kommunikation verstärken. 
Die Institutionen beginnen dann anstelle von  Wissen, anstelle von Bildung oder anstelle von Kunst 
– Ideologien zu produzieren. Sie verwandeln  sich, kurz gesagt, in Ideologiemaschinen. Der digitale
Medienwandel führt dazu, dass  die Verwaltungsapparate von Institutionen ihren Job nicht mehr 
erfüllen können, für den sie  geschaffen wurden und dysfunktional werden. Diese 
Selbstverstärkungsprozesse von politischer  Kommunikation haben meiner Ansicht nach
00:18:35
auch dazu geführt, dass der Anspruch  der documenta-Kunst politisch zu sein, eine 
vollkommen andere Form angenommen hat. Es  liegt in der Logik der ästhetischen Moderne, dass 
man auch soziale Bewegungen zur Kunst  erklären kann, so wie man Gebrauchsgegenstände zur 
Kunst erklären kann und dass diese politischen  Bewegungen dann auch als Kunst behandelt 
werden. Mit dem digitalen Medienwandel kommt es aber  zu jenen Selbstverstärkungseffekten 
politischer Kommunikation, so dass die politische Agenda von  politischen Aktivisten sich als neue 
Norm in den
00:19:23
Institutionen durchzusetzen beginnt. Und genau  dies ist in den letzten zwei 
dokumenten passiert. Politische Werte haben künstlerische Werte  überformt und dann fast 
vollständig verdrängt. Die Ausstellung hat sich dann in einer Weise in eine  politische Veranstaltung
verwandelt, dass sie eben auch nur noch in der Gesellschaft als politische  Veranstaltung 
wahrgenommen wird. Die Krise der documenta lässt sich also aus dem Zusammenspiel,  oder aus 
dem fatalen Zusammenspiel, von zwei Faktoren erklären. Die Documenta hält am Image  und am 
Markenkern einer Kunstavantgarde fest.
00:20:12
Das ist auch gut so. Von ihrer Erscheinungsform  her trifft man auf eine 
unästhetische, konzeptbasierte, genre-unspezifische Kunst, die  sich aber inhaltlich mit politischem 
Aktivismus verbündet hat und der politischen Agenda dieser  aktivistischen Bewegungen folgt. Die 
Inklusion von politischen Bewegungen in der Kunst folgt  zunächst einmal den Gesetzen der Kunst.
Das ist auch unproblematisch. In einer digitalen  Medienwelt führt diese Grenzüberschreitung zum 
politischen Aktivismus aber zu einer  Politisierung der Kunstinstitution selbst.
00:20:55
Das ist das zentrale Argument, so dass die Kunst  aus der Kunstkommunikation 
herausfällt und sich in politische Kommunikation verwandelt. Damit komme  ich zu meinem dritten
Punkt, zu den Optionen, wie sich unter diesen Umständen überhaupt noch  eine nächste Documenta 
planen lässt. Das ganze Ausmaß der Krise zeigt sich daran, dass es  schwerfällt, eine neue 
Findungskommission zu finden. Die documenta ist, mit anderen Worten, zum  Spielball der Politik 
geworden. Die schlechteste Option ist meines Erachtens, die documenta 16  einfach ausfallen zu 
lassen oder längerfristig
00:21:36
zu verschieben. Die politische Situation ist heute  derart volatil, dass es nicht sicher 
ist, ob es dann überhaupt noch eine nächste documenta geben  wird oder ob die finanziellen 
Ressourcen überhaupt noch bereitgestellt werden für sie. Die einzig  aussichtsreiche Option scheint 
mir hingegen, dass sich die documenta neu erfindet. Mein  Vorschlag wäre konkret, dass der 
Aufsichtsrat der documenta die Mitglieder der Findungskommission  nach einem ganz bestimmten 
Kriterium auswählt. Gute Kandidaten wären meines Erachtens  Schriftsteller, Kunstkritiker, 
Künstler oder
00:22:18
auch Kunstwissenschaftler oder Philosophen, die  sich in den letzten Jahren sehr 

deutlich für die Eigenständigkeit und für die Autonomie der Kunst  ausgesprochen haben und gegen
eine Vereinnahmung der Kunst durch Politik. Solche Stimmen gibt es  in der Kultur, man muss sie 
nur ansprechen. Und eine solche kunstaffine Findungskommission würde  dann mit Sicherheit auch 
einen Kurator finden, der einen Neuanfang der documenta aus dem  Geiste der Kunst wagt. Das 
hieße einerseits, dass eine solche documenta das Primat der  Kunst vor der Politik betont, und 
andererseits,
00:22:59
dass damit auch der politische Charakter  der Ausstellung abgewertet wird. Dies 
würde allerdings einen gravierenden Paradigmenwechsel  der documenta implizieren. Anstatt dass 
man wie bisher dem Paradigma der Einheit von Kunst und  Politik folgt, müsste eine documenta 16 
dann die Differenz von Kunst und Politik betonen und  das eben auch in dieser Ausstellung 
realisieren. Dieser Paradigmenwechsel impliziert, dass man  nicht länger am vorherrschenden 
Kunstbegriff der Documenta-Kunst festhalten kann, demzufolge Kunst  immer schon politisch sei. 
Stattdessen müsste man
00:23:40
die Frage stellen und das ist eben die Frage, die  bislang als selbstverständlich 
beantwortet galt, und die deswegen auch nie gestellt wurde, was  politische Kunst im Unterschied 
zu aller anderen Kunst eigentlich ist. Hier gibt es verschiedene  Möglichkeiten, diese Frage zu 
beantworten. Ich denke, wenn man politische Kunst als eine Kunst  begreift, die ein politisches 
Thema artikuliert, dann hat man es mit einem viel zu schwachen  Begriff von politischer Kunst zu 
tun. Das Schöne an der Kunst ist ja, dass  sie politische Themen aufgreifen kann,
00:24:19
aber dann in ihrer ganzen Ambivalenz,  Widersprüchlichkeit, Konflikthaftigkeit, in 
ihrer Abgründigkeit, in ihrer Tragik und  in ihrer Lächerlichkeit darstellen kann. Das kann nur die 
Kunst. Wenn das geschieht,  dann sind diese Themen für die Politik verloren und unbrauchbar, weil 
die Politik  eindeutige politische Haltungen benötigt. Deswegen schlage ich einen anderen Begriff
