von  politischer Kunst vor, nämlich einen engeren Begriff von politischer Kunst. Politische Kunst  
ist meines Erachtens Kunst, die ein politisches
00:25:02
Thema, die einen politischen Konflikt artikuliert  – dieser Konflikt muss bereits in 
der Gesellschaft als Konflikt diskutiert werden – und dann in Bezug  auf diesen Konflikt Partei 
ergreift. Abschließend noch ein paar praktische Ideen, wie so eine  Documenta am Nullpunkt 
eigentlich aussehen könnte. Das ist jetzt eine Art abschließendes  Gedankenexperiment. Es kann 
natürlich nicht darum gehen, politische Kunst zu verbieten  oder vorab zu zensieren. Das würde 
auch gegen die Kunstfreiheit verstoßen. Ich denke auch,  dass die documenta ihren generellen 
Anspruch
00:25:49
nicht einfach aufgeben könnte, gesellschaftlich  relevante Kunst auszustellen. Aber 
es gibt eben Möglichkeiten, die gesellschaftlich relevante  Kunst auszustellen, ohne dass sie 
politisch Kunst in diesem engeren Sinne ist. Zentral könnten dann  Werke sein, die politische 
Konflikte darstellen, aber in ihrer ganzen Ambivalenz und in  dem Sinne eben nicht politisch sind. 
Man muss auch nicht darauf verzichten,  politische Kunst im engeren Sinne auszustellen. Es geht 
nicht darum, die politische Künstler jetzt  aus der documenta rauszuhalten. Es geht eigentlich
00:26:34
nur darum, dass die Institution der documenta als  Institution politische Neutralität 
wahrt und das hieße eben für eine Ausstellung dass es durchaus  möglich ist, politische Werke im 
strikten und aktivistischen Sinne auszustellen. Es müssten  dann eben in dieser Ausstellung nur eben

beide Pole, also die beiden miteinander im Konflikt  liegenden Pole abgebildet werden, also zum 
Beispiel propalästinensische und proisraelische  Kunst gleichermaßen. Eine dritte Idee wäre, dass 
ich denke, dass das Thema der Kunstfreiheit  einfach so relevant heute ist, dass es auch in
00:27:23
einer Ausstellung reflektiert werden müsste.  Und man könnte eine Ausstellung so 
konzipieren, unter den vielen, die es in der documenta  gibt, dass man Werke zusammenträgt, wo 
Künstler angezeigt wurden oder verurteilt wurden oder im  Namen der Kunstfreiheit freigesprochen
wurden. Es wäre sicherlich interessant, sich dies einmal  zu vergegenwärtigen. Ich könnte mir aber 
auch vorstellen, und ich fände es auch interessant,  wenn Künstler Selbstzensur geübt haben, und 
dies zu dokumentieren. Das war jetzt aber  nur ein abschließendes kurzes Gedankenexperiment.
00:28:08
So viel documenta am Nullpunkt. Ich freue  mich auf unsere Diskussion. Vielen 
Dank.
The Crisis of Liberal Democracy 
00:00:00
Good morning. It should be self-evident that the  liberal democracies of the West are 
in a crisis today. However, there is disagreement as to what  this crisis consists of. For some, it lies 
in the triumph of populism. For others, it lies in the illiberalism of cancel  culture, and the BRICs 
states certainly pose a new threat. In my lecture, I would like to present a history  model that 
explains why all these various forms of illiberalism have a common origin. I will then go on to 
examine whether there is any  objective reason to hold on on the term liberal
00:00:46
liberal democracy at all. Terms such as liberal democracy can not only  change their 
meaning over time, but they can also fulfill different functions in  the self-description of a society. 
Liberal democracy in the postwar period was  initially a political idea. After the fall of communism,
it was transformed into an ideal. And today it takes the form of an ideology  depending defended 
and opposed by two political camps. When Churchill coined the metaphor of the  Iron Curtain in 
1946, this also had an impact on the concept of  democracy itself.
00:01:33
And Containment policy was pursued to contain the  influence of communism. The 
systemic conflict manifested itself in an  unchecked arms race. As a result, the idea of liberal 
democracy takes  the form of something imposed from outside by its enemies. The concept of 
democracy was designed as  a negative counter concept. The geographical border between the east 
and the  west was solidified into the guiding difference between the free and the unfree world. 
Accordingly, the democracies also referred to  themselves as Western democracies.
00:02:22
As a result, the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989  could also be seen as a global victory 
for the free world and for liberalism. This was reflected in the fact that the term  "liberal 
democracy" was now used more frequently than the term "Western democracy". Fukuyama 
interpreted this end of communism as the  end of history, insofar as the model of liberal 
democracies  without a genuine opponent could now spread worldwide. For the geopolitical 
strategists in the  USA, this was a clear signal to support the spirit of  world history in its eternal 
endeavor.

00:03:13
It was now possible to pursue a policy of regime  change more actively and 
overthrow unpopular dictators. The collapse of the Soviet Union was  used to expand NATO and the
European Union. The principle of "change through trade" was  applied, and the policy of open 
borders for goods,  services, people and capital was pursued. The liberal democracies also relied 
more on soft  power by trying to spread their values through non-governmental organizations and 
development  aid. And finally, liberal values were increasingly  enforced by legal means,
00:03:58
as Philip Manow explains in his excellent book  "Under Surveillance. The Definition 
of Liberal Democracy and Its  Friends." Manow even speaks of the "birth of liberal democracy" in 
the 1990s,  because the understanding of what a liberal democracy actually is is now changing  
fundamentally. In the past, democracy was primarily understood to  be an electoral democracy, 
which essentially means that a government can be  voted out of office through elections. Political 
scientists began now to argue that such  electoral democracy was an inferior form of
00:04:45
democracy. Instead, it was now assumed that a full  fledged democracy would have 
to take the form of a liberal democracy. According to Manow,  the essence of this newly conceived 
liberal democracy lies in a "constitutional revolution". On the surface, this is manifested in a 
worldwide  proliferation of constitutional courts. In reality, however, the constitutional courts are  
being used more and more to remove certain issues from the political political agenda and from the  
ballot. For example, even if a majority of the population  votes out the current migration policy,
00:05:33
constitutional courts are preventing politicians  from implementing the will of the 
voters. Democracies reacting usually to this kind of  democratic deficit with a populist revolt. The 
change in the function of constitutional  courts is a vivid illustration of why liberal democracy has 
become an ideal. The constitutional courts before 1989 were  supposed to stabilize democracy and 
protect it from relapsing into dictatorships. In contrast to this, the constitutional courts  after 1989 
are increasingly being used as a
00:06:21
political instrument to transform society into a  better place. During the Cold War, the
constitutional courts  provided a political frame. After the fall of communism,  they increasingly 
served the purpose of political framing. However, the Constitutional Revolution  described by 
Philip Manow was only one factor among many that transformed the idea of liberal  democracy into
a positively formulated political project. The constitutional revolution was not the  reason, but at 
most the keystone for this new  understanding of democracy.
00:07:08
The decisive factor was that the concept of  democracy had lost its external reference
point after the end of the Cold War. Or rather, it no longer had any basis in the  reality of socialism, 
and existed only as a narrative,  a narrative that could be idealistically rewritten as needed. Under 
these conditions,  the idea of democracy was transformed into a term without distinction, and that 
is,  into an ideal. A political ideal can only integrate a society as  long as it seems realistic. The key 
premise of this ideal was that  globalization would actually transform the world
00:07:57
into a global liberal democracy. It is precisely this assumption that has since  proved 
to be a major historical miscalculation. With the election of Trump and Brexit in 2016,  the end of 
history itself came to an end. The return of history marks the beginning of the  crisis in which 
liberal democracies find themselves today. Ultimately,  global liberalism has proven to be a failed 
ideal, or rather a failed utopia,  and has consequently been transformed into an ideology. 
Globalization has not spread the model  of liberal democracy worldwide,
00:08:44
but instead created a new class of globalization  losers who vote for populist parties. 
Globalization has not produced a single world  society, but a hostile multipolar world. The idea of 
"change through trade" has not led to  a wave of democratization in China, but has instead created a 

new anti-liberal  superpower. And the institutionalization of a  nondiscriminatory society in the 
form of diversity, equity and inclusion programs has given  rise to an illiberal cancel culture. Last 
year, I published this book,  "Ideology Machines.
00:09:31
How Cancel Culture Works",  in which I described this new kind of illiberalism in 
liberal democracies in detail. Ultimately, the populist revolt triggers a  political realignment, 
meaning the parties have to align themselves with  a new line of conflict and have to change their 
programs. Accordingly, a general distinction can  be made here between a communal, a 
communitarian, and a cosmopolitan camp,  each of which reacts differently to the globalization 
induced crisis. Focusing on traditional traditional values such as  community,
00:10:16
nation, religion, and family,  the communitarian camp rejects not only the utopian 
project of liberal democracy,  but, in its conservative core, the whole idea of liberal democracy 
itself. The argument of national conservatism in the USA  is that liberalism destroys precisely those 
traditional values of family,  nation and culture that form the foundation of any democracy. This is a
serious argument. It is a clear ideological position that the  cosmopolitan camp currently has little to
oppose. National populism is calling into question the  entire political paradigm of the
00:11:05
post-reunification area,  namely that crossing boundaries and open borders are 
fundamentally positive things. And This is a philosophical remark. The observation does not refer to
a specific  political political conviction, but to a structure of our grammar with which we  observe 
the world. It is a general attitude to all kinds of border,  boundaries and limitation issues. Like avant
garde art the political system believed  in this. In this historical a priori. It must be stated that the 
utopian ideal of  liberal democracies has produced a threefold
00:11:53
illiberalism. First, illiberalism,  which can be seen in the cancel culture or in the 
freezing of bank accounts or in hate speech laws  of political opponents. Second, the illiberalism 
that national  conservatism contains as part of the populist movement. And thirdly, there are illiberal
superpowers that have emerged under the ideal of global, a global liberal world. We are therefore 
dealing with a perfect crisis of  liberal democracies, in which their friends and their enemies are  
equally involved.
00:12:40
When big ideas fail, it's time for philosophy. The nature of philosophical theories is 
that you  don't immediately know what they are good for. That's what happened to me with this 
book  "Art-Love-Religion", which will be published in two weeks. In this monograph, I'm actually 
trying to close a  theoretical gap in lumen Niklas Luhmanns social theory. And in view of the crisis 
of liberal  democracies, I have noticed that I can also derive a  meta-political argument from that 
theory, namely that democracies are inherently liberal  political systems.
00:13:25
And I think this is an important argument in a way  it can shape political discourses. 
Luhmann's central idea is that the structure of  social society changed in the 15th century, and that 
this change can be described by a  different coding of communication. European societies in the 
Middle Ages were  hierarchically differentiated societies with a pope or an emperor on the top of 
the social  pyramid. This top position also gave the highest,  these highest representatives absolute 
power to define what could be spoken and thought in a  society.
00:14:10
The various spheres of communication,  of economy, of law, of knowledge, of justice
and beauty were  fuzed together through their positive values. It was therefore not possible to say 
what was true  or just or beautiful, independently of the Catholic Church or of the  Christian faith. 
Triggered by the innovation of the printing press, the Reformation, and the development of 
empirical  sciences, the modern era then saw a gradual decoupling of  the communication areas. The
more the integrative power power of religion  in society diminished,

00:14:54
the more the different communication areas were  able to specialize. Over the 
centuries that led to to the formation of  autonomous functional systems such as the science system, 
the economic system,  the legal system, and the political system. These social systems used their 
own programs and  criteria to decide whether a statement is true or false, or whether an action is 
lawful or unlawful. In the scientific system,  for instance, these programs take the form of theories 
and methods. In the legal system,  the programs manifest themselves in the form of
00:15:41
laws and lawsuits. In each of these autonomous  autonomous spheres of 
communication, however, a logical problem occurred. In the legal system, the question arose as to  
whether is it is just or unjust to distinguish between justice and injustice. The solution lies in the 
self-acceptance of the  code, which is represented in system theory by this  figure of re-entry. The 
distinction between justice and injustice  marks itself with the positive value of justice. In 
Luhmann's theory, all functional systems are  coded according to this pattern of
00:16:29
self-affirmation. There is a code with a positive  and a negative value, and this code 
performs a re-entry on the positive  side. The re-entry defines that the sentence of the  excluded 
third party applies in each communication field. A scientific statement must be able to be  true or 
false, and cannot claim to be divine or just or beautiful  in order to to be accepted by scientists or by
the scientific community. This in turn leads to the  exclusion of the subjects with their feelings, 
thoughts, perceptions, and wishes from these  highly specialized specialized communication
00:17:23
systems. The functional systems force the subjects  to disregard themselves, which 
causes the typical modern experience of  alienation. So much for Luhmann's system theory,  which 
convincingly describes the functional differentiation of society up to the 19th century. At the 
beginning of the 20th century,  however, a development began that can no longer be adequately 
depicted using automatic orthodox  system theory. There is a specific inclusion of subjective  
experiences in the communication processes of art,
00:18:12
love, and religion. For this reason,  I have coined the term "human media". Human 
medium. My enhancement of Luhmann's system  theory consists of the proposition that the coding 
of communication in such human medium underwent a  fundamental change. The code does not 
follow a re-entry,  but it follows, so my suggestion, a re-exit. The rule states, for example,  that the 
binary code of art and non-art should not be confirmed, but rather negated and thus rejected  as a 
distinction. This is exactly how the avant-garde worked  consciously trying to include non-art into 
the art
00:19:02
system and into the art communication,  for example by declaring everyday objects 
to be works of art. If the art system follows this  logic, which only accepts art that is incomparable 
with  all established art styles, genres, isms and concepts,  then truly new art only manifests itself in 
singular artworks. All other human media also work  according to this logic. Modern society not 
only sets free the potential  for singular art communication, but also produces singular love 
relationships and  singular relationships with God.
00:19:49
Precisely because the human media makes the  perception, the perceptions, the 
feelings and thoughts of  humans bservable. observable to others. They also function as media  
media of extreme subjectivation. I illustrated this rather abstract chain of  thought with four 
European arthouse movies. In Ruben Östlund's film Force Majeure,  for example, a married couple 
can only save their relationship  by undertaking such a re-exit in their love communication. The 
movies also show that liberal  democracies have learned to observe these
00:20:37
sophisticated, sophisticated communication  strategies. Such archetypes of singular 

subject. Such archetypes of singular Subjectivity are then  taken up by popular culture and can 
spread throughout society. You can see at a glance that  the German Basic Law essentially codifies 
the functional differentiation of modern society. This relates to the principle of the separation of  
powers, that is, the autonomy of the legal system and the  autonomy of the political system. 
However, functional differentiation also explains  why modern societies adopt democracy as a form 
of
00:21:24
government. The switch to a binary code of  "government and opposition", between 
which one can freely choose how power is  distributed in society, already constitutes the political 
system of a  democracy. In addition, the autonomy of the religious system  is enshrined in the print 
in the principle of Religious freedom as formulated in article four,  and the autonomy of the science 
system and the art system is reflected in the principle of freedom of  science and in the principle of 
freedom of art in
00:22:02
article five. Article two of the Constitution is  not merely a political or a religious 
statement. The "free development of personality" is a right  that ratifies the development towards 
singular subjectivity, which the human media enable in a in  the mode of the re-exit. The binary 
coding of politics through a re-entry,  and the binary coding of human media through a re-exit are 
based on the same principle of  functional differentiation. However, since the differentiation of 
society  produces both an autonomous political system and
00:22:49
leads to a singular subjectivation in human media, there is indeed an intrinsic 
connection between  democracy and liberalism based on this meta political analysis. We can begin 
to explain why the project of  exporting democracy was doomed to failure. You can replace a 
dictatorship with a democracy in  in the short term, but if there is no liberal democracy in the  
country, such an imposed democracy will collapse again. A critical mass of people is needed who 
have a  genuine self-interest in their own
00:23:36
self-development, and accordingly also regard the  autonomy of art, religion and love
as a positive value. Otherwise, there is no reason among the population  why society should not be 
integrated via religion or via ideology. Without a minimum level of liberal culture  democracies 
revert to autocracies and dictatorships. The Weimar Republic failed,  not least because liberalism 
was not strong enough in the population, and Russia's eternal relapse  into dictatorships is a 
consequence of the fact
00:24:20
that the liberal bourgeoisie has been imprisoned,  murdered and driven out of the 
country time and time again . In all these courses,  In all these cases, pre-modern communication 
relationships are  reestablished in which the different communication spheres of society are merged 
by their positive  values. This study of liberal democracy does not want to  side with the friends of 
liberal democracy, whom Manow speaks of, nor does it want to side  with the enemies, who gave 
rise to populism. From our perspective, perspective,  both political positions are right and wrong at
00:25:11
the same time. A dysfunctional term of liberal  democracy is defended by the 
cosmopolitan camp and fought against by the communitarian camp. That's why the political 
discourse is an absolute  mess today. The bottom line of my analysis is that liberal  democracies are 
suffering from the consequences of their own hubris. After the victory of the Cold  War, there was 
indeed a belief that the highly  sophisticated construct of liberal democracy had no limits. However 
almost all disputes in today's  political struggles concern precisely such limits.
00:25:59
The communitarian camp can fall back on tradition, or on natural limitations of 
nation states,  of sexes, or of cultures. The cosmopolitan camp,  which is much more interested in 
self-realization, subjectivation and singularization is has to  construct these limitations itself. In a 

word, this camp needs a term of democracy  that focuses on auto-limitation. That would be my 
suggestion. Thank you very much for your attention.

Postmoderne Weltbild Grammatik 
00:00:01
Good afternoon, and thank you very much for the invitation. I would like to convince
you in my lecture that postmodernism has come to an end and that this has something to do with the
revaluation of the concept of community. I will develop this thesis in three steps. First of all, I will 
explain the shimmering concept of postmodernism through the concept of worldview-grammar. 
Secondly, I would like to present a theory of political realignment that can be used to show that this 
postmodern worldview-grammar no longer fits the current political situation in which we find 
ourselves.
00:00:50
Postmodernism is no longer an agent of liberalization, but is turning into its opposite 
and legitimizing a kind of illiberalism today. And this, my third point, is precisely where the irony 
of history is revealed once again. Discourses arise, and discourses fade away. Discourses are 
constituted by distinguishing themselves from a previously dominant discourse with the help of a 
guiding difference and thus defining their external boundaries. They then begin to differentiate 
themselves within these self-imposed boundaries and then gain a certain range and sometimes even 
dominance in the culture.
00:01:35
And finally, discourses lose their basis of plausibility again and begin to question and
reflect on their own constitutive boundaries and also to transgress them, so that they finally 
disintegrate again as discourse formations. I developed this three-phase model in the book 
"Aesthetic Experience. A discourse analysis". This discourse on aesthetics, which has been 
dominant in Germany over the last 50 years, developed in the early 1970s through a critique of the 
previously authoritative aesthetics of Adorno, Gadamer and Heidegger.
00:02:19
The argument for the demarcation was that it would be a matter of the so-called 
"aesthetics of truth", which would not think aesthetic experience autonomously, but 
heteronomously, and that one would therefore have to return to Kant's Critique of Judgment. For 
here we would have a kind of proto-theory of aesthetic experience. This tripartite discourse model 
can be applied to all discourse formations, both large and small. And it can of course be applied to 
the largest conceivable discourse formation, namely worldview-grammars.
00:03:00
In this respect, I will now use this discourse model to analyze the postmodern 
worldview-grammar. The constitutive idea of postmodernism or the constitutive ideas emerged in 
the 1960s and 1970s, in the arts, in literature and also in philosophy. Derrida developed the concept 
of deconstruction, according to which the claim to truth of every statement can be deconstructed. 
Foucault worked out the main features of a discourse analysis according to which all discourses are 
integrated into power dispositives.

00:03:38
These ideas later developed into what could be called the two constitutive principles 
of the postmodern worldview-grammar. Firstly the theorem that all knowledge is a cultural 
construction and that no binding truth claims can be formulated at all, and secondly the theorem that
all knowledge always already fulfills a power function and follows power interests. Strictly 
speaking, however, these two postmodern principles are a very simplistic interpretation through 
which the complex texts of Derrida and Foucault have been brought into the form of guidelines, 
maxims and principles.
00:04:23
The authors themselves never formulated their ideas in such general terms, and they 
would also have rejected the attribution that they were postmodern. In our discourse model, such 
cultural generalizations are an indication that the postmodern worldview-grammar has entered its 
differentiation phase. In this schematized form, the ideas of Derrida and Foucault were now able to 
spread across broad areas of culture and penetrate many different disciplines of knowledge. In this 
way, these theorems began to structure social communication like a grammar.
00:05:09
In a word, they became the worldview-grammar of liberal democracies. The crucial 
question here is why Derrida's and Foucault's ideas were selected by Western culture and were able 
to reinforce themselves over decades. So why were these two postmodern theorems able to prevail 
over thousands of other ideas circulating in the philosophical discourses of the 1980s? My thesis 
here would be that it was the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 that created the plausible conditions for 
postmodern texts to crystallize into a postmodern worldview-grammar.
00:05:57
Up to this point, the systemic conflict between socialism and capitalism was the hard 
guiding difference that shaped the idea of social reality. Fukuyama now spoke of "the end of 
history", insofar as it was assumed that liberal democracies, and thus also the principles of 
economic and cultural liberalism, would prevail as a global political order. The two postmodern 
theorems were thus selected by the liberal democracies of the West because the concept of freedom 
was no longer politically restricted, questioned and limited by competing world views.
00:06:44
Under these conditions, postmodern principles win out such as "anything goes", "all 
knowledge is a social construction", "claims to truth are claims to power", "beauty is in the eye of 
the beholder", "everything is text", "The author is dead", and also "the private is political". All these
priciples maximize social connectivity. Due to their successful use, these maxims have become the 
historical a priori of a postmodern worldview-grammar. Postmodernism has been declared dead for 
decades, which you can believe or not.
00:07:22
In our model, the criterion for a worldview-grammar having passed its zenith and 
entered its reflection phase would be that its plausibility conditions have radically changed. This 
can be justified by recourse to a political science theory, which brings me to my second point: the 
political realignment. This diagram shows the party positions for the 2017 federal election as 
determined by the Federal Election Compass. It is a graphic representation of the political space as 
it has existed in Germany for decades.
00:08:10
In his standard model, a political space has two dimensions of conflict, one economic
and one cultural. And these can then be mapped in a coordinate system with a horizontal left/right 
distinction and a vertical progressive/conservative axis. The initial hypothesis, which I take from 
political science, is that Germany, like the UK and the USA, is now also experiencing a political 
realignment, i.e. a realignment of the party system and a reconfiguration of the political space. 
Political realignments occur rarely but regularly in democracies.
00:09:05
They are based on fundamental changes in the political attitudes of voters. And these 
changes in attitude are caused by major social, cultural and economic transformations. Most 

recently, Stephen Davies updated this theory in 2020 in his book "The Economics and Politics of 
Brexit. The Realignment of British Public Life". I take the basic idea from Davies, but vary it at two
crucial points. Firstly, I will not apply the theory to the political space of Great Britain, but of 
Germany. And secondly, this realignment theory takes on a specific form for me, namely the form 
of a reversibel figure.
00:09:56
The idea of this flip model is that political realignment can be represented by a re-
designation of the two axes that constitute the political space. And then, of course, two questions are
crucial: How has the political space been configured so far? And the second question is: What has 
changed or how has this configuration, that is the meaning of the axes, changed today? The political
system in democracies must permanently resolve two basic conflicts. First, an economic conflict: a 
free market economy generates wealth that is distributed according to market laws, which means 
that it is distributed unequally, so that it has to be redistributed to some extent.
00:10:50
And this conflict is played out in the political system through left-wing and right-
wing parties, and it is also represented through them. Second, a basic cultural conflict in 
democracies is between those who have an interest in preserving tradition and morality and those 
who want to overcome and leave behind traditional ways of life and moral concepts. Democratic 
elections ensure that a majority of society can live with both the economic and cultural changes and 
that a collectively binding decision is made for a certain period of time.
00:11:32
This is how the political space has been configured up to now. However, political 
scientists have suspected for around 15 years that this political space is being reconfigured because 
a new structural conflict is manifesting itself in liberal democracies. This conflict can be named in 
different ways. On the one hand, there is the conflict between the globalization winners and the 
globalization losers. Globalization appears to be the driving force behind the realignment and is also
leading to the rise of populist parties.
00:12:17
On the other hand, this conflict is also described as a conflict between the 
Somewheres and the Anywheres. The Somewheres live in places where the ICE passes by, and the 
Anywhere sit in these ICEs and drive past these places. This distinction articulates a specific feature
of this new line of conflict. This conflict is evident and manifests itself in the difference between 
city and countryside and between center and periphery. The winners of globalization are well 
educated. They live in the big cities, in the metropolises, they get the well-paid jobs, they are 
flexible on the labor market and can move between the metropolises of this world.
00:13:09
The globalization losers remain tied to their places of residence. They live 
somewhere in the provinces. A third common distinction for describing this new line of conflict is 
to distinguish between a cosmopolitan and a communitarian camp. This ties in with the 
philosophical discussions on communitarianism. And that is also the point of contact, or my point of
contact, with our conference topic, with the limits of community that Helmuth Plessner talks about. 
Because the Somewheres are dependent on communities, they also tend towards communitarian 
views that emphasize the value of community.
00:13:57
The Anywheres have detached themselves from communities and accordingly 
represent a cosmopolitan world view. The Realignment thus leads to the distinction between society
and community being used differently as a code of preference in political communication. In the 
cosmopolitan camp, society functions as a positive value within the distinction between society and 
community, and in the communitarian camp, community is the positive value that is endowed with 
a preference over society. I have tried to make this a bit clearer here with this system-theoretical 
designation technique.

00:14:44
As I said, the idea for my flip model is that I asked myself the question: How do the 
meanings of the axes that constitute the political space change when the new conflict is now 
between globalization winners and globalization losers, between anywheres and somewheres and 
between the cosmopolitan and the communitarian milieus? The left/right difference is now being 
reshaped by a preference for national and global economic policy. This is because many economic 
problems and changes are shaped and caused by global transnational economic processes, such as 
free trade agreements, labor migration or European financial policy.
00:15:35
The decisive economic issues are no longer negotiated in one's own country, which is
why there is now a national pole on the economic horizontal axis. This national pole is critical to 
the EU. It is critical of the euro, and it is also protectionist with regard to its own national economy. 
And on the other hand, we now have a pole, you could call it the global pole, which is in favor of 
deepening EU cooperation, saving the euro and is fundamentally in favor of free trade. Even the 
cultural conflicts are no longer intra-cultural conflicts.
00:16:28
They no longer take place between progressives and conservatives in one and the 
same culture, but the new cultural conflicts are globalization-induced identity conflicts between 
those who are developing a cosmopolitan way of life and those who see new work and life 
opportunities in globalization and can also discover them for themselves. And those, on the other 
side, who are stuck in their villages, their small towns, their districts, but who are also rooted there 
and genuinely depend on functioning communities.
00:17:14
Now we have to deal with another name for the cultural axis. It is in addition crucial 
that the realignment reverses the order of the axes. Until now, the economic axis has always been 
the primary axis. The Realignment makes the cultural axis the primary axis and the economic axis 
the secondary one. And that's why we have a culture war in the first place. So on the one hand, we 
now have a pole that is about a self-seeking cosmopolitan identity, i.e. a habitual lack of attachment 
to one's own culture and, above all, to the nation state into which one was born.
00:18:04
People are trying to distance themselves from it. And on the other hand, we are 
dealing with a preference for found national identities, in principle for found identities in general. 
But national identity is particularly politically relevant. This is about a habitual attachment to one's 
own national culture and to the nation state. You are attached to the culture into which you were 
born and which you cannot simply transcend and leave behind in your own life because you don't 
have the appropriate university education, because you don't speak many languages and because 
you don't have the economic means to move around.
00:18:52
In this constellation, migration automatically becomes the number one topic of 
conflict. If the coordinate system changes in this way, the political system will be realigned. The 
new line of conflict is initially emerging between all the established parties on the one hand and the 
AfD on the other. But this graphic here is no longer calculated like the Federal Election Compass, 
but is now a graphic projection by me. So in a way, I have now distributed these parties as I believe 
they would locate themselves in this new coordinate system, if you look at their election programs 
and their rhetoric.
00:19:44
The Left, the CDU, the Greens and the FDP are now in the same quadrant, where the 
value of a transnational identity is more or less preferred. Accordingly, people advocate a refugee 
policy, which could then be articulated as a "welcoming culture". And it was precisely this policy 
that gave rise to the populist protest and thus made the AfD strong. For a long time it was the only 
party in this communitarian quadrant, where a national identity policy is coupled with an anti-EU 
and national economic policy.

00:20:44
The phases of political realignment are short, unstable phases, and they are 
ambivalent for everyone involved. There are now two coordinate systems in society, but also for the
parties and in the media and, above all, in the minds of voters. And this is precisely what leads to 
reversible figures, which my flip model is intended to depict. The tipping point and the ambivalence
can be illustrated very nicely if you flip back and forth between these two models. You can see that 
the axis of conflict is also turning as a result of these new designations of the axes that constitute the
political space.
00:21:39
So much for the state of political realignment in Germany in 2020. I published this 
flip model in the June issue of Merkur, i.e. in 2020, you can still access this text now. However, I 
would like to take the opportunity today to update the model. Now, four years later, we find 
ourselves in a slightly different political situation. After losing the last elections, the CDU has 
moved. She has left the Merkel position and moved more and more towards the coordinate origin of
the new political space.
00:22:26
It wants to be the party of the center and signals its ability to form coalitions on all 
sides. However, this is unlikely to succeed under the current political conditions, or will only 
succeed with great difficulty. A characteristic feature of today's political space is that it is divided by
a firewall. And this firewall naturally restricts the CDU's space for maneuver extremely. Since 2022,
there has also been a new conflict dividing the political camps, namely the war in Ukraine. It is 
reflected in the economic axis, in which there are parties that are more in favor of aid to Ukraine 
and others that are against it.
00:23:15
And on the cultural axis, this is reflected in the fact that certain parties are showing 
solidarity with Putin and others with Zelensky. However, the greatest momentum in the political 
arena was generated by the split of the BSW from the left. In all likelihood, the left will disappear 
from the political map. The BSW, on the other hand, like the AfD, has moved into the 
communitarian quadrant. It is now very clear that the former left/right distinction has been replaced 
by a new political reference point.
00:23:56
Because now you have two parties in one and the same quadrant, one of which has 
its origins in the extreme left and the other in the extreme right. They come together in the same 
political camp. Not only are they similar in their party platforms, and sometimes also in their 
rhetoric, but I think that they are actually potential coalition partners in the medium term. This is 
what the political space in Germany will look like in the fall of 2024. In my opinion, how the 
realignment will continue depends largely on what policies the CDU will offer.
00:24:45
This is because the CDU is the least politically committed of all the parties, including
in terms of its personnel. And it will also determine what shape the political space in Germany will 
take in the future. With the help of this realignment model, three scenarios can already be played 
out today. What I would like to demonstrate, simply to illustrate how you can work with this model 
and that it can fulfill a certain orientation function. If the CDU continues to form a coalition with 
the Greens and the SPD, it will have to move back to a Merkel position in order to establish itself as
a cosmopolitan people's party.
00:25:31
This actually seems counterintuitive, but I don't think it's out of the question that 
Angela Merkel will ultimately triumph and the CDU will establish itself as a cosmopolitan people's 
party in this cosmopolitan quadrant. The price for this scenario is that the AfD would once again 
receive a massive influx of CDU voters and perhaps even replace the CDU as the strongest political
force in the entire country, as has already happened in eastern Germany. The second scenario is 
basically the scenario that we have now seen in the last state elections in eastern Germany.

00:26:16
On the one hand, the CDU is moving into the communitarian quadrant, so that 
alliances of convenience can be formed with the BSW, as otherwise there is no government option 
at all as long as at least the firewall exists. This scenario is also highly risky, because an alliance 
with an ex-communist could once again lead to voters defecting to the AfD. At the same time, 
cooperation with the BSW will normalize the AfD and move the firewall a little further. What I 
have drawn here, that would  be the third scenario, that is considered more or less unthinkable 
today.
00:26:59
Namely that the CDU is establishing itself as a communitarian people's party and is 
trying to enter into alliances with the AfD in the future. If there is a real power option for the AfD, 
then it will, in all likelihood, disarm rhetorically, back away from its most radical demands and try 
to marginalize the extremists in its own ranks or exclude them from the party. Or they will step into 
the second row for tactical reasons, as has now been observed in the Netherlands, and pull the 
strings from there.
00:27:35
This scenario is ultimately based on a script that has already been implemented in 
many European countries. Just think of Italy, Switzerland, Austria, the Netherlands, etc. In 
principle, however, the political system is paralyzed today and it will not be able to operate its way 
out of this paralyzed state on its own. The situation is only likely to improve once the political 
realignment has been successfully completed and there is a cosmopolitan and a communitarian 
political camp that, firstly, take each other seriously as political opponents and do not deny each 
other legitimacy, and secondly, that there is a common concept of social reality so that the different 
political camps do not live in different worlds, as is typical of realignment times.
00:28:47
If such a realignment does not succeed, then the political polarization that is 
characteristic of such times can also lead to a structural catastrophe, i.e. a collapse of the political 
system of liberal democracies. But I myself am optimistic, because realignment is actually a 
mechanism used by democracies to pre-empt and avoid revolutions. And this brings me back to my 
topic of worldview-grammar. After all, there will be no reformatting of the political space without a 
reformatting of the pre-political space.
00:29:32
A successful realignment also requires a new self-description of society, and that 
means a reformatted worldview-grammar. The spirit of postmodernism has turned into its opposite 
today. And that's why I'm talking about the irony of history in my last point. When the political 
arena is transformed, parties become disoriented, as do voters and the media. Voters are now 
interested in other issues than those addressed by the parties in their manifestos. And in such phases 
of upheaval, activist movements begin to play a special role in a society.
00:30:26
They are beginning to gain an orientation function, because they have a clear idea of 
what left-wing politics should actually be and what right-wing politics should actually be. In these 
times of upheaval, political activism therefore fulfills a role model and an orientation function in the
political arena. For a decade now, the New Left and the New Right have been focusing on identity 
politics. And this left-wing and right-wing identity politics are at odds with each other precisely 
because of this realignment.
00:31:12
And they are also creating the strong political polarization in society at the moment. 
The political realignment can only be successful if this temporary political activism in politics or on
the political stage loses its importance again. Ideally, the parties will end up taking more contrary 
but moderate positions on identity politics and distance themselves again from the activist 
movements they have followed for a while. However, and this is my crucial argument, this is hardly
possible or extremely difficult under a postmodern worldview-grammar.

00:31:58
A successful realignment of the political space also requires a transformation of the 
pre-political space, which defines how a society communicates about itself. As I said, the 
catchwords, the slogans and the simple theorems of the postmodern worldview-grammar were first 
extracted from the complex and ambiguous postmodern primary texts. Only in the form of guiding 
principles such as "All knowledge is a social construction" and "All knowledge fulfills a power 
function" can the difficult-to-read texts by Derrida and Foucault unfold their emancipatory potential
in culture at all.
00:32:43
Today, on the other hand, it is precisely these principles that legitimize anti-
democratic and anti-liberal ideologies. The best example of this is Trump, who is the face of hyper-
affirmative postmodernism. He is the trickster that Till Dembeck described him as. Trump simply 
takes the phrase "everything is social construction" literally and does not shy away from any lie or 
half-truth if it benefits him politically. The fact that Trump's political style is successful with half of 
the American population is due to the very postmodern worldview-grammar in which his voters 
were socialized.
00:33:26
And this socialization took place not only at universities, but also in popular culture, 
on talk shows and in reality shows such as The Apprentice, whose host was Donald Trump. 
Nowhere is it easier to shape social awareness than in a place where it is entertained. If propositions
about reality are perceived from the outset as a social construction, then all arguments in one's own 
world that contradict one's own world view can also be declassified as fake news. There is no longer
a shared reality on which an argumentative debate could be based; instead, all statements can be 
interpreted as political statements and, accordingly, fought against as political statements.
00:34:22
But postmodern knowledge is also being politically instrumentalized in the left-wing 
camp today. If there is a consensus in institutions that it is power interests that ultimately play the 
decisive role in the scientific disciplines and that power interests are ultimately expressed in the 
scientific disciplines, then it is difficult to say anything against cancel culture. Cancel Culture is 
also ultimately based on a hyper-affirmation of the postmodern worldview-grammar. I have just 
published the book "Ideology Machines", which argues that digitalization leads to a general self-
reinforcement of political communication in institutions.
00:35:08
Due to digital feedback loops, the administrative apparatuses of institutions are now 
themselves coming under moral and political pressure. Instead of intrinsic values such as education,
research or art, they are increasingly beginning to represent political values, which tends to lead to 
the development of a cancel culture. This is by no means censorship, but rather self-censorship, 
when values such as artistic freedom, academic freedom or freedom to teach are abandoned by the 
institutions themselves.
00:35:42
My proposal, which I make in this book, is to build ideology disrupters into the 
institutions so that political communication can be dimmed down. However, a society that is 
socialized in a postmodern way ultimately lacks good arguments for keeping political 
communication out of cultural and educational institutions. Anyone who uses a postmodern 
worldview-grammar to perceive, to thinks and to communicates, must find the demand for political 
neutrality from cultural and educational institutions pointless, since all communication is always 
already politically constituted and dependent on power.
00:36:29
One could say that the concept of postmodern worldview-grammar here becomes a 
second-order discourse analysis, a discourse analysis of Foucault's discourse analysis. Today, the 
postmodern worldview-grammar destabilizes both the liberal and the democratic momentum of 
liberal democracies. Liberal democracies are a special case of democracies. The democratic 
momentum is weakened by the maxim "All knowledge is a social construction", precisely because 

it legitimizes the rhetoric of populists. The liberal moment is then called into question by the maxim
"All knowledge is an expression of power relations".
00:37:16
While the liberal democracies of the 1990s and 2000s found the highest form of self-
expression in a postmodern culture, today its hyper-affirmative worldview-grammar that has sunk 
into the social subconscious strengthens anti-liberal and anti-democratic forces. In addition to this 
internal dysfunctionality of the postmodern worldview-grammar for liberal democracies, there is 
another external reason why postmodernism has come to its end, namely a new systemic conflict 
between liberal democracies on the one hand and illiberal autocracies on the other, which will in all 
probability lead to a new Cold War that will determine life and political life for decades to come.
00:38:14
In principle, you can also define the end of postmodernism. It falls on the 24. 
February 2022, when Russia attacked Ukraine with a major offensive. In view of this new systemic 
conflict, the postmodern worldview-grammar is losing precisely the plausibility basis that it had 
gained with the end of the first Cold War in 1989. The postmodern worldview-grammar was 
selected by the liberal democracies in 1989 because the principle of freedom seemed unlimited and 
unchallenged. After a good three decades, the breaking point of postmodernism is now visible, 
which consists of an internal and an external threat.
00:39:07
A political realignment is a stress test for any democracy. For intellectuals, for artists,
for scientists and also for philosophers, however, realignment times are very productive times, 
because they are now faced with the task of analyzing and reworking their own worldview-
grammar. Thank you very much.

in the following second part i would like to outline very briefly a model of aesthetic modernity on 
the basis of which this contradiction can be discussed and analyzed the core idea of this model is 
that in the 20th century an internal differentiation process took place in the art system and that the 
major scissors of modern art history can thus
00:14:11
be reconstructed this process consisted in a decoupling of three essential components
of art communication the work the medium and the concept in the fine arts of the 19th century these
three components were tightly coupled in the 21st century there is art where these components are 
only loosely coupled to each other the driving force behind this history of differentiation is the 
ability of the modern arts to negate all conventional notions of art until the 19th century the medium
of the fine arts intensified the expression and
00:14:56
experience of beauty at the beginning of the 20th century the negation of the medium

cubism or expressionism determine the way in which the aesthetic material is to be arranged in an 
artwork in the next step the negation of the artwork constitutes the art of the historical avant-garde 
whose most radical manifestation is conceptual art here art is reduced to the perceptual 
representation of a concept
00:15:39
strictly speaking conceptual art can be defined by an isomorphism principle

isomorphism the concept of the work is given by the title namely to erase a drawing by de kooning 
and what one can perceive is exactly such an erased drawing postmodern art overcomes the avant-

garde by negating a strong taboo of the avant-garde
00:16:24

represented in the model with a gap

the summer school here for some years now a strong politician has been emerging in the arts and so 
i would like to ask straightforwardly the question what is political art what do we mean when we 
speak of political art what does it mean when the arts have become politicized and what it is the 
reason for this boom of political art in the recent years my plan is to start with three possible 
definitions of political art and my
00:00:43
question is always how feasible or unfeasible are these definitions and what is the 
cognitive power of these terms and based on that conceptual groundwork i will then analyze and 
discuss various examples of political art the first definition for political art is art is always political 
art the term is often used by artists who see themselves as political activists from this perspective 
the initial problem simply disappears if art is always political art then it makes no sense to ask how 
and why art is becoming highly
00:01:31
politicized today for our discussion this definition of political art is an inappropriate 
term my second definition is the obvious one namely art addresses a political issue in this sense van 
gogh sunflower paintings would not be political art this notion captures all the works of art that 
articulate some social issue but this commonplace notion of political art is in my opinion to weak i 
want to suggest a strong definition of political art art takes sides in a political conflict this concept 
includes the previous
00:02:18
concept of political art because an artwork can only take sides in a conflict if it 
articulates a political conflict the criterion is therefore stronger the third notion of political art has 
two aspects political art articulates a political conflict and in addition it takes sides in this conflict 
this concept of political art assumes that one cannot arbitrarily define what is political but that the 
distinction between the political and the non-political is first and foremost established in the 
political system
00:03:01
most social issues are not political issues they must first be communicated as such in 
the political dis system political art can only take sides if there are political parties or at least 
political movements that take a contrary position with regard to this one conflict this is a conceptual
groundwork for the analysis of political art but it is helpful to change the first definition of political 
art but it's not always political art but art can be always politicized and here i mean art can always 
be interpreted or presented

00:25:10
rather a structural problem has emerged in recent years that i would like to call 
digitally amplified group polarization or amplified group polarization for short what you see here is 
a general model of an institution such as a university or a museum or an art academy an institution 
has an administration that has to make specific decisions such as which artist to collect and which 
artists to show as with normal group polarization digitally amplified group polarization also 
requires a homogeneous
00:25:50
value environment as we discuss discussed in relation to the alice solomon college 
universities and art scenes usually share a cosmopolitan value system ranging from moderate to 
radical positions and there must be political activists who want to push these institutions in a certain
direction so that are the ingredients for the model under these conditions sooner or later there will 
be conflicts between activists groups and and administration the administration has to represent the 
intrinsic values of the institution such
00:26:30
as the search for truth in universities or artistic freedom in the arts thus decisions 
must be made about whether a political demand is in the institution's interest or not before the age 
of new media administrations could easily handle such conflicts and protest and protect the self-
interests of their institutions this was still possible even if there was group polarization within the 
institutions for example a large group of employees taking an activist position this could happen in 
the digital age this becomes more

00:27:12
difficult although for example the concept of microaggression is certainly not

accusations most of which are made anonymous miss anonymously the fear of reputational damage 
is great and the fear of reputational damage is
00:27:53
as already mentioned the main reason for grip polarization this creates a positive 
feedback loop in the institutions as a result of which group polarization now extends to the 
administration instead of being able to decide on the appropriateness of a political demand 
administrators are now drawn into the political discussion themselves in other words they lose their 
political neutrality to do their job digital amplified group polarization can thus lead to the 
politicization of entire institutions
00:28:35
in extreme cases such such institutions can then become dysfunctional which in 
relation to the arts would mean that artistic points of view become subordinate to political goals the 
mighty thesis is that there is a structural conflict between spheres the spheres of art and politics the 
autonomy of art as it was worn by the avant-garde can no longer be taken for granted in this respect 
my last example concerns the relationship between art and politics as a whole we can see here

00:30:04

becomes an art of counter politicization
00:30:47
this brings me to the end of my lecture my main aim was to analyze the phenomenon 
of political art with the help of a constellation of examples on the one hand it should become clear 
that there are different variants of political art and politicized art and on the other hand that there are
two mechanisms of politicization of the arts first it is a consequence of a political realignment that 
takes place today in the society and on the other hand this is a consequence of the digital revolution 
which leads to the new
00:31:25

the art system in the coming years one must even reckon with art institutions collapsing under these 
circumstances and one final remark i would like to point you to this essay in letter international in

a reflexive notion of music uh hello hari we are welcome uh we're very pleased to

very much for being here at least virtually and please uh we can we can start uh your lecture now 
thank you and thank you very much for the nice introduction and good afternoon to everybody 
today at my third lecture at the

00:00:55
contemporary music festival contrasts in with i would like to present to you a model 
how the idea of new music has changed to due to the digital revolution of music and the thesis is as 
the title suggests the concept or the notion of art music has become reflexive the two previous 
lecture on tron ryan watson's opera and on conceptual art and music will fit into this model in 2012 i
published the book the digital revolution of music and music philosophy and in my opinion music 
philosophy is not really an academic discipline
00:01:41
philosophical thinking arbutin about music is only needed in those rare historical 
moments when the entire musical culture is undergoing a serious change and as a result it becomes 
necessary to rethink the whole concept and the whole notion of art music my thesis which i had 
presented in 2008 in a thought experiment was that exactly this is the case at the beginning of the 
21st century because a change from a literary literary to a digital music culture is taking place many
of the arguments in the book were
00:02:26
hypothetical eight years ago and have now become almost self-evident above all 
there are many new works in your music in which this change to what digital music culture 
manifests itself and accordingly it is much easier to say today in what sense the notion of music has 
changed if one understands music philosophy in this sense it has three different problems to solve 
first it must reconstruct the old paradigm that has been valid up to now let us call it paradigm one 
and secondly music philosophy has to describe
00:03:13
the transformation mechanism that leads to the present paradigm shift which is today 
triggered by the digital revolution and thirdly music philosophy is faced with the task of designing 
the the following paradigm paradigm 2 being created so what does it mean to reconstruct a 
paradigm and more specifically a paradigm of art music in principle it is a matter of analyzing the
